  • Adjudication. Hear appeals from the Inquisition. Render judgment on inter-noble disputes that touch theology. Confirm or void the noble Houses' marriages, inheritances, and legitimacy claims.
  • Resource control. Authorize Skyguard deployments above the divisional level. Sign continent-wide trade decrees. Tax the noble Houses through tithe-equivalent obligations.
  • Public face. Maintain the visible presence of the Twelve through scripted appearances — Majesty's appearances at court ceremonies, Mercy's annual hospice blessings, Mortis's grave-counting at year's end.

docs.title: Structure
docs.text:
• The High Throne. A single seat occupied by Majesty. Currently held by Hadrian of House Auremont. The Throne speaks for the pantheon when the pantheon speaks publicly.
  • The Council of Twelve Voices. Twelve seats, one per M. By doctrine, each Voice is the god themself. In practice only four of the Twelve reside in Aurion and attend in person — Majesty, Mercy, Malice, and Muse. The other eight reside elsewhere on the continent, and for each the Court has appointed a Senior Speaker to hold the Voice's Council seat as proxy. The seat remains the god's; routine attendance is the Speaker's. The Voice of Madness has been vacant for six years — the prior Speaker died and the goddess has not chosen a successor.
  • The Quorum of Sanctified Houses. Twenty-three noble Houses with hereditary seats. Vote on land, trade, and noble-precedence matters.
  • The Clerks of Aurelium. Career bureaucrats. Run the Court's day-to-day operations. Not titled, not voting, indispensable.

docs.title: Public figures
docs.text:
• Hadrian Auremont (Voice of Majesty). The High Throne. Public face of the pantheon. Speaks at all major occasions.
  • Senior Speaker Theron Vethraine (Speaker for Memory). Holds Memory's Council seat in her absence — Memory's incarnation resides in Moonbough. Senior archivist and informal historian; has held the seat for forty-one years. Speaks slowly. Knows where every body is buried, often literally.
  • High Chamberlain Brienna of House Caltherine. Runs the Palace's operational staff — schedules, security, ceremonial logistics. Reports directly to the Throne.
  • Senior Speaker Wulfrik Brynhalla (Speaker for Might). Holds Might's Council seat in his absence — Might's incarnation resides in Khalvar Hold. Former Khalvar warlord, promoted to the Council eleven years ago.

docs.title: Methods
docs.text:
• The Sealed Address. When the Court announces something significant, it is read simultaneously in all five capitals at sunset. Mandatory attendance for sanctioned clergy.
  • The Pilgrimage Calendar. Determines which sites are blessed, when, by which Voice. Cities live and die by it.
  • The Censor's Wax. Books, pamphlets, and printed materials touching the Twelve, the Pit, or the Skyfall require Court-issued wax-seals to circulate legally.
  • The Quiet Conversation. Senior Court members do not threaten in public. They invite the noble in question to tea. Tea is a ranked invitation: morning tea is informational; afternoon tea is corrective; evening tea is final.

docs.title: Reputation
docs.text: Universally respected, deeply feared in specific ways, and almost never disobeyed in public. The Court's grip on Aetherion is doctrinal, ceremonial, and procedural at once. Most Skyborn defer without thinking about it; the rare ones who don't usually have a tea invitation in their future.

docs.title: Order of the Pure Flame
docs.text: The Court's militant arm for shard-related crimes. Where the Skyward Inquisition handles heresy, Ground-sympathy, and doctrinal threats, the Pure Flame handles shard-bearers — those who possess, use, or transport relics without sanction. They guard the Court-authorized vaults, lead shard-recovery crusades, and pursue unauthorized bearers as fugitives across the five nations.

docs.title: What they do
docs.text:
• Guard. Authorized shard vaults across the five nations are protected by Pure Flame detachments — typically a senior officer plus four to eight sworn brothers and sisters. The largest detachment is at the Order's Aurion chapterhouse.
  • Hunt. Unauthorized shard-bearers — anyone holding, wearing, or wielding a shard without an Aetheric Collegium license — are crusader targets. The Order pursues them across the continent, with sealed Court warrants giving them jurisdiction in every nation.
  • Recover. Lost relics — particularly the named historical shards — are the Order's ideological mission. Generations of recruits join with the hope of being the one who recovers a master shard.
  • Burn. A Pure Flame crusader who fails their hunt, defects, or is found to have hidden a recovered shard for personal use is burned — ceremonially put to death by their own brothers. The ritual is old. It still happens.

docs.title: Structure
docs.text:
• The Flame-Marshal. Apex commander. A single seat. Currently held by Marshal Aldric Veteraine of House Veteraine — a cadet branch of House Auremont, distantly related to the High Throne.
  • The Sworn Captains. Eight in total — six regional commanders (Aurion, Khalvar Hold, Cogbarrow, Moonbough, Greenmere, Tether's End) plus two roving Crusade Captains who travel with active hunts.
  • Sworn Brothers and Sisters. About 400 active across the continent. Full members of the Order, oath-bound, armored, named.
  • Postulants. About 150. The pipeline. Five-year postulancy; many wash out.
  • The Burning Chamber. Not a rank — a place. The vaulted room beneath the Aurion chapterhouse where ceremonial executions happen. Existence is widely known; access is restricted to Sworn members.

docs.title: Public figures
docs.text:
• Flame-Marshal Aldric Veteraine. Sixteen years in the seat. Speaks formally. Believes the Order's mission correctly.
  • Sworn Captain Brenna Stormbrand. Roving Crusade Captain, currently active in eastern Ankarum and the Khalvar trade routes. The Order's best hunter; eleven recoveries in nine years.

docs.title: Methods
docs.text:
• The sworn warrant. Pure Flame warrants are open-jurisdiction. They overrule local law and Inquisition warrants both. A Pure Flame Captain can compel a Skyguard division to assist if necessary.
  • The flame-iron. Every Sworn brother and sister carries a small, blessed brand-iron that resonates in the presence of an active shard. Imprecise but reliable in the gross sense.
  • The hunt-banner. When the Order declares an active hunt, a banner is unfurled at the chapterhouse. Public; visible across the city. The banner stays until the hunt resolves.
  • The Greater Burning. The Order's central rite. Held twice a year in the Burning Chamber. Outsiders are not present.

docs.title: Reputation
docs.text: Devout, dangerous, and respected. The Order is small enough that most Skyborn never meet a Sworn brother; large enough that everyone knows what the flame-sigil on the shoulder means. Their hunts make news. Their executions do not.

docs.title: Skyguard
docs.text: Highcrown's standing army. The continent's largest organized military force — about twelve thousand active personnel under arms — operating airships, conventional infantry, cavalry, siege engineers, and the largest single garrison structure on Aetherion. Reports nominally to the High Throne through the Voice of Might's seat on the Council of Twelve Voices.

docs.title: What they do
docs.text:
• Garrison. Standing garrisons in Aurion (the largest), Khalvar Hold, Moonbough, Cogbarrow, Greenmere, and Tether's End.
  • Patrol. Inter-nation borders, the major trade routes, the Anchor's upper approaches.
  • Operate airships. About thirty airships of various classes, docked principally at Aurion's Skyhold and Khalvar Hold's Aerie.
  • Train. Continuous training cycles across the major garrisons. Specialist schools by location (airship crews at Khalvar, cavalry at Moonbough, artillery at Cogbarrow, medical at Greenmere).

docs.title: Structure
docs.text:
• The Lord Marshal. Apex; single seat. Currently Lord Marshal Caspian Brightcrest (human, late fifties; nine years in seat).
  • The Marshalry. Six Marshals beneath the Lord Marshal, each commanding a division: Aurion Field, Khalvar Field, Northern Field, Western Field, Southern Field, and the Aerial Marshal (airship corps).
  • Colonel-Generals. Twelve regiment commanders.
  • Captains. About 80 company commanders.
  • Rankers. The rank-and-file, about ten thousand strong.

docs.title: Public figures
docs.text:
• Lord Marshal Caspian Brightcrest. Brother to the Aetheric Collegium's Magister Primus; the Brightcrest line produces scholars and generals in alternating generations.
  • Aerial Marshal Iverra Stormbrand. Commands the airship corps; cousin to Brenna Stormbrand of the Pure Flame and Senior Speaker Bertran Stormbrand at the Court.
  • Marshal Korunna Halrandsdotter. Commands the Khalvar Field garrison; coordinates with the Keelwrights on Ankarum's joint operations.
  • Captain Sevran Truefont. Commands Aurion's First Company; ceremonial duties at most Court functions.

docs.title: Reputation
docs.text: Respected, professional, slightly slow to mobilize. The Skyguard's training is famously thorough and slightly old-fashioned. The patrol rotation means most communities see Skyguard personnel regularly but rarely the same faces. The institution carries the prestige of being the army that protects the continent; the bureaucracy of being the largest peacetime force on Aetherion has worn a certain amount of dashing off its actual culture.

docs.title: Skyward Inquisition
docs.text: The Majestal Court's enforcement arm. Hunts heresy, Ground-sympathy, and forbidden shard lore through informants, closed trials, and quiet disappearances. Feared, respected, and — by most ordinary Skyborn — considered necessary.

docs.title: What they do
docs.text:
• Investigate. Maintain a continent-wide network of paid informants, sealed case files, and standing surveillance details on registered persons of interest.
  • Detain. Take suspects into custody on Inquisition warrants. Hold them in the cells beneath the Aurion headquarters or in district lockups across the five nations.
  • Try. Conduct closed trials before three-judge panels of senior Inquisitors. The accused are heard; defenders are limited; verdicts are not appealed except to the Majestal Court itself, which has reversed seven cases in the last thirty years.
  • Punish. Sentences range from indentured service to permanent exile to execution. Public executions are rare; most sentences are carried out quietly.
  • Confiscate. Recover unauthorized shards and turn them over to the Aetheric Collegium or the Order of the Pure Flame, depending on classification.

docs.title: Ranks
docs.text:
• Inquisitor — field operative, recent oath, squad member.
  • Inquisitor-Captain — squad leader, five to ten years' service. Authorizes raids.
  • Inquisitor-Warden — specialized roles (vault chief, archive, evidence). Rarely in the field.
  • Inquisitor-Commander — senior field administrator. Authorizes major operations.
  • Senior Inquisitor — the apex public role. Speaks for the Court on doctrine.

Each nation has a regional Inquisition administration; all five report up to Aurion. Aurion HQ runs continent-wide priorities, sets doctrine, and operates the master case-file archive.

docs.title: Public figures
docs.text:
• Senior Inquisitor Cassia Velaspar. The apex. Decades of exemplary service; the public face of the Inquisition's "stern but fair" doctrine.
  • Inquisitor-Commander Veska Orval. Cassia's deputy. Runs daily operations at Aurion HQ. Skyguard veteran.
  • Inquisitor-Warden Tomas Kestrin. Aurion vault chief. Bookish, scrupulous, treats artifacts with more care than people.
  • Inquisitor-Captain Sevran Marrek. District officer for the Low Quarters and mid-quarter periphery of Aurion. A career professional, present at most public Inquisition actions in those districts.

docs.title: Methods
docs.text:
• The notebook. Captains and above keep personal case-files in clothbound notebooks. Lost notebooks trigger internal review.
  • The whistle. Standard issue. Heard for hundreds of feet through walls. An Inquisitor in trouble gets reinforcements very fast.
  • The bell-rope. Larger Inquisition installations have concealed bell-ropes that summon the district patrol.
  • Sealed warrants. Warrant-bearer Inquisitors have priority over local law and can compel cooperation from Skyguard, Crown Assembly, and most nobles. Only the Court can override a warrant; few are willing to try.

docs.title: Reputation
docs.text: Most Skyborn never deal with the Inquisition directly, and don't want to. The Inquisition's public position is procedural and unsentimental: enforce the Inquisition Policy, close cases cleanly, document everything. The institution's grip on Aurion society is heavy enough that fear of attention is a real social force, not merely a moral one.

docs.title: Archivists of Moonbough
docs.text: The Sylvari scholarly order that maintains the Great Library of Moonbough — the largest accumulated archive on the continent. The Library's stacks descend through the canopy city's roots into chambers whose age the Archivists themselves cannot fix. The Archivists claim an unbroken record reaching back to the earliest generations of the Founding, and few scholars seriously dispute their reputation for thoroughness.

docs.title: What they do
docs.text:
• Catalog. The Library's core function. Every book, scroll, broadside, tablet, and oral history they can acquire is logged, cross-referenced, and shelved according to a system only senior Archivists fully understand.
  • Preserve. Damaged texts are restored. Disintegrating ones are copied. Texts in dead languages are translated and the originals stored in climate-controlled vaults.
  • Restrict. Not everything in the Library is publicly accessible. The deeper one descends, the more restricted the holdings. The deepest stacks — the Sealed Boughs — are open only to a handful of the institution's most senior figures.
  • Authenticate. When the Court or a noble House needs a historical document validated, they send to the Archivists. The authentication carries continent-wide legal weight.
  • Teach. The Archivists run the Sylvari conservatories — the formal scholarly training pipeline that produces most of Aetherion's senior scholars, artists, and historians.

docs.title: Structure
docs.text:
• The Lady of the Library. A single seat. Currently held by Lady Saerith-wen-Maeloria. Senior advisor to the Sylvari Council of Boughs on historical matters.
  • The Wardens of the Boughs. Seven senior Archivists. Each oversees a Bough — a thematic division of the Library (Cosmology, History, Magic, Languages, Genealogy, Geography, Apocrypha).
  • Archivists. About 120 full-credentialed scholars. The body of the institution.
  • Apprentice-Scribes. About 300, mostly young elves but some half-elves and rare humans.
  • The Stewards of the Sealed Boughs. Three positions. Always elves, always over 400 years old, always nominated by the Lady herself. They walk the deepest stacks. They speak rarely.

docs.title: Public figures
docs.text:
• Lady Saerith-wen-Maeloria. The Lady of the Library. The continent's senior historian.
  • Warden Therennor Sablefern (Warden of Apocrypha). The keeper of the Library's apocrypha — disputed histories, fragmentary and uncatalogued texts, and correspondence held under Court restriction. Quiet, dry, rarely seen in public.
  • Warden Iolyth-wen-Aerinwë Silverleaf (Warden of Cosmology). The most publicly visible Warden; advocates for wider access to the Library's holdings.
  • Steward Olwyne (Steward of the Sealed Boughs). Walks the deepest stacks. Speaks once a month at the Stewards' Council.

docs.title: Methods
docs.text:
• The Cataloging Tools. Archivists carry a small cataloging-stylus — brass, inscribed with their personal sigil. The stylus reads runes that ordinary eyes miss; it identifies provenance, authenticity, and resonance on items it touches.
  • The Bough Ascent. Annual ceremony. A scribe presents original work to the Lady; if the work is judged worthy, the scribe is raised — promoted to a higher tier of stack access. Genuinely meritocratic.
  • Inter-library exchanges. The Archivists trade documents with the Aetheric Collegium. The trade is the lifeblood of new acquisitions.
  • The Sealed Bough rule. Nothing leaves the Sealed Boughs. Nothing. Stewards may transcribe; they may not remove.

docs.title: Reputation
docs.text: The Archivists are, by some margin, the most respected institution outside Aurion. Their authentication is unimpeachable; their scholarship is the standard; their senior members are quietly consulted by every Court in the five nations. The Library itself is one of the wonders of Aetherion.

docs.title: Sylvari Council of Boughs
docs.text:
The deliberative body of Sylvaris. A confederation of the seven elder Sylvari Houses, convening in Moonbough at the Great Trunk — a living-wood assembly chamber suspended in the canopy above the city's roots. Each House holds one "Bough" — a seat with one vote. Decisions require the consensus of all seven.

The Council is, by Sylvari design and by elven temperament, slow. Ordinary business takes months. Major decisions take years. Outside observers — particularly the Highcrown Court — find the pace exasperating. The Council does not adjust.

docs.title: What they do
docs.text:
• Confer. Convene three times a year at the Great Trunk. Sessions last weeks. Each Bough Speaker brings their House's standing items; the agenda is set by Council convention; the conversation runs at the Council's preferred pace.
  • Adjudicate. Resolve inter-House disputes over land, lineage, marriage, and ritual precedence. The Council's rulings are binding on its member Houses and respected by the other nations.
  • Speak with one voice externally. When the Council can reach consensus, it speaks to the other nations through the Speaker-of-the-Boughs — a rotating five-year office held by one of the seven Bough Speakers in turn. When consensus is not reached, Sylvaris simply does not speak.
  • Advise the Library. The Lady of the Library (Saerith-wen-Maeloria) has standing right of address at the Council and is heard on historical and cosmological matters. The Library's senior position is structurally above the Council on these questions.
  • Welcome envoys. Receive ambassadors from the other four nations. Maintain elven embassies in Aurion, Khalvar Hold, Cogbarrow, and Greenmere.

docs.title: The seven Boughs
docs.text:
• House Silverleaf — long-established scholar-house. Allied closely with the Archivists.
  • House Sablefern — the Apocrypha-keepers; quietly powerful within the Library.
  • House Aelinmoor — the moon-pool ritualists; observatories and prophecy.
  • House Thaliel — the youngest of the seven Houses, elevated three centuries ago.
  • House Cael-Thain — the most geographically isolated, based in the far western forests.
  • House Dawnglen — the dawn-tradition Sylvari, oldest astronomical lineage.
  • House Eldengrove — the eldest House by formal precedence; quiet, sparse, ancient.

docs.title: Public figures
docs.text:
• Speaker-of-the-Boughs Maelorin-ion-Saelath of House Eldengrove. Currently holds the rotating Council leadership; entering the fourth year of a five-year term.
  • Speaker Saerith-wen-Maeloria of House Maelora — the Lady of the Library, standing advisor on historical matters.
  • Bough Speaker Iolyth-wen-Aerinwë Silverleaf — speaks for House Silverleaf; also the Archivists' Warden of Cosmology.
  • Bough Speaker Therennor Sablefern — speaks for House Sablefern; also the Archivists' Warden of Apocrypha.

docs.title: Reputation
docs.text: Slow. Careful. Externally inscrutable. The Council's outside reputation — ostensibly united, persistently divided — is partly accurate (the Boughs disagree often) and partly a deliberate cultivated impression (Sylvaris finds it useful to be underestimated). The Council's actual track record on the major continent-wide decisions of the last five centuries is consistent: when the Sylvari finally speak, they speak in the direction of preservation, restraint, and long horizons.

docs.title: Riftwatch
docs.text:
Tether's End's standing watch. Sworn to the Court, they guard the Grand Anchor, police the village, and enforce the prohibition on unauthorized descent to the Pit. They are also — by general agreement among everyone who lives at Tether's End — widely understood to be partly compromised. The Salvage Syndicate operates without serious interference; the Riftwatch's monthly "watch contribution" is a known quantity. The Court declines to investigate.

The arrangement, in the open: a watch-officer takes the month's pouch while a run is lowered over the edge. Everyone at Tether's End knows.

docs.title: What they do
docs.text:
• Guard the Anchor. A small specialist sub-unit — the Anchor-Wardens — works the structure itself: walking the lower scaffolds, watching the bell-rope alarms, and denying access to the restricted heart of the structure.
  • Police the village. Patrol the rope-walks. Break up fights. Recover stolen goods. Investigate the occasional fall or knife-fight.
  • Enforce the descent prohibition. Pat down departing pilgrims who wander too close to the lower ledges. Inspect cargo destined for the lower lift-shafts. Process descent permits when the Court issues them (which is rare).
  • Report to the Court. Quarterly. The reports are short.

docs.title: Structure
docs.text:
• The Watch-Captain. A single seat. Currently held by Halnir Stonebeard, a dwarven officer in his late fifties who has held the seat for twenty-three years.
  • Watch-Lieutenants. Three. Each commands a shift (dawn, day, night) and a roster of about fifteen Riftwatchers.
  • Riftwatchers. About forty-five rank-and-file. A mix of dwarves, humans, halflings, and a handful of others.
  • Anchor-Wardens. Six specialists assigned to the Anchor itself. Engineer-trained.
  • Aspirants. Eight to twelve in training at any time; six-month aspirancy.

docs.title: Public figures
docs.text:
• Watch-Captain Halnir Stonebeard. Twenty-three years in the seat. Tether's End's senior law-enforcement officer.
  • Lieutenant Velrin Threadbare. Day-shift commander; ambitious; widely expected to be the next Captain.
  • Lieutenant Drennik Ironward. Night-shift commander; Halnir's old friend.
  • Senior Warden Iolyth Brassglint. Senior Anchor-Warden; the only person in the village who walks the lower scaffolds during storms.

docs.title: Reputation
docs.text: Honest about being compromised, which is itself a kind of integrity. The Riftwatch don't pretend to be the Skyguard. The accommodation between watch and smugglers is old enough to feel like part of the village's working order rather than a scandal. Outsiders find it shocking; locals don't.

docs.title: Salvage Syndicate
docs.text: Tether's End's smugglers' guild — such as it is. The Syndicate operates lift gangs at the Grand Anchor, runs informal cargo routes that the Riftwatch politely doesn't notice, and brokers goods that fall outside the Court's sanctioned trade. Officially they do not exist as a recognized organization. Practically they are the single largest employer in Tether's End and the working logistics layer the village runs on.

docs.title: What they do
docs.text:
• Lift work. Anchor crews pulling crates, cargo, and salvage off the ledges below the village. The official-looking work; mostly legitimate freight handling.
  • Quiet runs. Move goods between Tether's End and the surface in defiance of the descent prohibition. Most cargo is mundane (leathers, ores, herbs that grow only below); most of the village pretends not to know.
  • Brokerage. Match buyers and sellers across the legitimacy line. A noble House quietly wants something Tether's End can supply; the Syndicate finds a willing supplier and takes a cut. The brokerage office sits above the Salt Wharf.
  • Protection. The Syndicate has its own enforcers. Disputes inside Tether's End — debt, theft, broken contracts — usually settle through the Syndicate before they reach the Riftwatch.

docs.title: Structure
docs.text:
There is no formal hierarchy. There is:

  • The Boss. A single informal title held by acclamation; currently Cassio Underbrook, a halfling who has been at Tether's End for thirty years and Boss for the last twelve. The title is held until the holder loses the room.
  • Crew bosses. Five. One each for lift work, quiet runs, brokerage, enforcement, and the Salt Wharf cargo flow.
  • Crew members. Roughly eighty active across the five crews.
  • The Boss's table. Six chairs at the back of the Anchor and Lantern (the tavern the Syndicate informally owns). Cassio holds court there three evenings a week.

docs.title: Public figures
docs.text:
• Boss Cassio Underbrook. Pragmatic, well-liked, soft-spoken. The Syndicate's senior figure for twelve years.
  • Lift Gang Boss Korbin Stoneveil. The most publicly visible Syndicate figure to outsiders; runs the official-looking lift work.
  • Brokerage Manager Lethaiyn Greenways. Runs the office above the Salt Wharf. The Syndicate's polished face; can pass as a legitimate factor in any of the five capitals.

docs.title: Reputation
docs.text: Outside Tether's End: criminal, dangerous, beneath notice. Inside Tether's End: the people you actually pay rent to. The Syndicate is more orderly than its outsider reputation suggests — partly because chaos is bad for trade, and partly because the Anchor itself is a place where carelessness gets people killed.

docs.title: Halfling Tithe League
docs.text:
The cooperative association of Tumblebrook farms and orchards that coordinates the continent's food distribution. Membership is voluntary; almost every halfling farm of consequence has been a member for generations. The League negotiates grain prices, allocates seasonal surpluses to nations that have undershot their harvests, runs the Greenmere Granary (the continent's largest grain reserve), and quietly takes a small administrative cut on every league-distributed sack.

Most Skyborn outside Tumblebrook know the League only by its results — that there is bread in every market every winter even when the harvests have been uneven. The mechanism is largely invisible to them, and the League prefers it that way.

docs.title: What they do
docs.text:
• Coordinate. Set seasonal prices and allocation targets for grain, dairy, autumn fruit, and root vegetables across all five nations.
  • Distribute. Move surplus from over-producing districts to under-producing ones, through the League of Feathered Roads' caravan network.
  • Reserve. Maintain the Greenmere Granary as the continent's emergency food store. Sufficient in normal years for one full harvest's gap.
  • Tithe. Members contribute a fixed proportion of their harvest to the League's reserve and operational fund. The tithe is the League's only real revenue.
  • Adjudicate. Resolve member disputes over land, water-rights, and labor exchanges. Tithe League adjudication has continent-wide standing within halfling culture.

docs.title: Structure
docs.text:
• The Tithe Council. Twelve senior figures, elected by the membership for staggered eight-year terms. Currently chaired by Mistress Pip Goodbarrow (a cousin to Pearl Goodbarrow of the festival hall, though that connection is not what got her elected).
  • District Stewards. One per major Tumblebrook district. Manage the regional allocations.
  • The Granary-Master. Single seat, currently held by Master Otto Threshfield. Runs the Greenmere Granary.
  • Caravan-master liaisons. Coordinate with the League of Feathered Roads on shipping schedules.
  • Member farms. Several thousand across Tumblebrook.

docs.title: Public figures
docs.text:
• Mistress Pip Goodbarrow. Chair of the Tithe Council. Two years into a third elected term.
  • Master Otto Threshfield. Granary-Master. Has run the Greenmere Granary for nineteen years.
  • Steward Brook Reedwater. Senior District Steward for the central plains; the League's most visible field officer.

docs.title: Reputation
docs.text: The Tithe League is the polite face of halfling competence. Universally respected by the other nations' merchants; quietly indispensable to the Court's food policy; deeply embedded in the rhythms of Tumblebrook life. The League's reputation for diligent, unflashy work is largely accurate — and is, also, the cover for a great deal of useful institutional discretion.

docs.title: League of Feathered Roads
docs.text:
The halfling caravan guild that runs the continent's everyday civilian transit infrastructure. Skybridges between cities, ropeways through canyons, overland caravan routes, river boats, ford crossings, mountain mule trains. Most Skyborn live and die on this network — it is the only realistic way for an ordinary person to get from one nation to another.

The League is, technically, a cooperative of halfling caravan-master families. It has been operating in something like its current form for over two centuries. Membership is hereditary in practice and meritocratic in form; nearly every senior halfling caravan family belongs.

docs.title: What they do
docs.text:
• Run the routes. Six major continent-spanning routes, dozens of regional connections, hundreds of local feeder lines. Schedules are seasonal; reliability is the League's brand.
  • Maintain the infrastructure. Skybridges, ropeways, river docks, way-stations. The League's masons, riggers, and bridge-keepers are the continent's specialist craft layer for civilian transit.
  • Coordinate with the Tithe League. Move grain, dairy, and seasonal produce alongside passengers. The two Leagues are formally separate but operationally inseparable.
  • Provide messenger service. The League's route-runners — single halflings who travel a fixed route on horseback or by light cart — carry mail, parcels, and small urgent goods between every city. A route-runner can move a sealed message across the continent in about three weeks.
  • Train. Caravan-masters apprentice from childhood. The training is family-run, multi-generational, and intensely practical.

docs.title: Structure
docs.text:
• The Roads Council. Nine senior caravan-masters, elected by the membership for staggered six-year terms. Currently chaired by Master Penn of the South Road.
  • District Wardens. Six, one per major continent-spanning route. Coordinate operations along their route.
  • Caravan-masters. About two hundred, each running a regular caravan with a household of riggers and drivers.
  • Route-runners. About sixty, the League's messenger corps.
  • Member households. Several hundred halfling caravan-family households across Tumblebrook and beyond.

docs.title: Public figures
docs.text:
• Master Penn of the South Road. Chair of the Roads Council. Forty years a caravan-master; the most-traveled halfling currently alive.
  • District Warden Daisie Northridge. Senior District Warden for the northern Khalvar-Aurion route.
  • Bridge-Keeper Mort Underbrook. Senior infrastructure officer at the Greenmere-to-Aurion skybridge complex.

docs.title: Reputation
docs.text: Indispensable, reliable, slightly grumbled-at. Every nation depends on the League; every nation also wishes the League had a less independent culture. Caravan-masters are famously unflappable, occasionally insubordinate, and universally trusted with the cargo. The League's senior figures are some of the continent's quiet wisemen — they've seen everywhere, met everyone, and judged most of it.

docs.title: Geography & Drift
docs.text:
Aetherion is a massive floating continent suspended in the sky. It is stable enough that most inhabitants treat it as the entire civilized world. It has forests, mountains, rivers, farms, ruins, cities, and weather.

It is not naturally stable. It drifts on "chosen currents" of high-altitude wind and arcane resonance, nudged by colossal gyres and rune-engines. The gnomes' great machine — the Crowngear, beneath Cogbarrow — allows them to angle Aetherion's drift by subtle degrees. Sabotaging the Crowngear risks sending the continent into the forbidden ground.

docs.title: The lay of the land — the five nations
docs.text:
Aetherion as the guild cartographers draw it: Aurion at the center, the four realms at the compass points, and Tether's End clinging to the southern rim where the land falls away into open sky.

Aurion and Highcrown sit at the center of the continent, on its highest plateau; the four other nations ring the capital, and the great roads radiate outward from Aurion to each. Rivers run down from the central heights toward every shore.

  • North — Ankarum (dwarves). The high country: snow-capped mountains and rune-cut halls, with Khalvar Hold seated among the peaks.
  • West — Sylvaris (elves). The great forests, where the canopy-city of Moonbough rises among ancient trees.
  • East — Glymswick (gnomes). The industrial reach, where Cogbarrow sits atop the Crowngear that steers the whole continent's drift.
  • South — Tumblebrook (halflings). The lowland breadbasket — fields, orchards, and river-country — that feeds the continent, and whose caravans bind it together.
  • The southern rim — Tether's End. Beyond Tumblebrook's farmland, at the continent's southern edge, stand the Grand Anchor and the only sanctioned way down to the surface.

docs.title: See also
docs.text: • travel-and-flight.md — how people move across it.

docs.title: The Grand Anchor
docs.text:
When Aetherion pauses its drift, it moors at one place: the Grand Anchor at Tether's End. The Anchor is a vast structure of black metal that does not rust, etched with symbols no scholar recognizes. It is older than the dwarves' runes, older than the gnomes' turbines, older than any standing record.

No one may approach the Anchor proper without royal license. The bribed guards look away. The structure itself is too dense, too hot, and too strange to be approached safely without preparation; the Anchor crews who work it have shorter careers than other tradesmen at Tether's End for reasons everyone agrees not to discuss.

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: The Anchor's documented function is straightforward: it holds Aetherion steady when the gnomes shut the Crowngear down. Its means are not documented, and no Skyborn engineer has ever satisfactorily explained them.

docs.title: The Great Chain
docs.text:
From the Anchor's underside descends the Great Chain — a colossal length of the same black, un-rusting metal, links a house could pass through, etched with the same unreadable symbols. It falls past the lowest ledges of Tether's End and down into the cloud-gulf, vanishing toward the surface far below. It is the literal tether the town is named for.

The Chain sings: a deep, continuous tone, felt more than heard, that rises and falls as the drifting continent pulls against its mooring. Tether's End lives by its moods — "the Chain runs low" for calm, "she's singing high" for strain and weather — and reveres it as the continent's other great marvel. Travelers touch it for luck; the few who come this far swear they can feel the Pit answering through the iron.

docs.title: What we don't know
docs.text: A great deal. The Anchor's age, origin, and full functions are all matters where the scholarly position is "we don't know" — said with varying degrees of comfort. The Aetheric Collegium has been studying the Anchor for centuries and has produced a great deal of careful, hedged, ultimately inconclusive scholarship.

docs.title: The Pit
docs.text:
Below Aetherion lies the Pit — a hellscape of corruption, poisoned air, twisted creatures, and demonic powers. Its inhabitants — Groundborn — are tainted: skin marked, eyes wrong, bodies warped. They consort with the Fallen Powers; they hate the Sky; they would, given the chance, drag Aetherion down into their ruin.

Descent is heresy. Trade with Groundborn is treason. The Anchor exists to hold Aetherion steady, never to invite passage.

Most of what Skyborn know about the Pit comes from sermons, festival readings, and the children's rhyme — The Pit Below, recited in every nursery. The fuller Majestal Court teaching is in the Underworld Primer.

docs.title: What's reliably known about it
docs.text:
• The air is described as poisoned. Travelers who have approached the descent shafts report difficulty breathing.
  • The terrain is described as scarred. Reports from authorized descents (long-lapsed) describe pits, blackened plains, and ruined structures.
  • The inhabitants are described as numerous. Estimates vary; the Court does not publish figures.
  • The Fallen Powers — the Vs the Twelve once defeated — are described as present there. Their worship is one of the most serious heresies on Aetherion.

docs.title: What's not known
docs.text: A great deal. Almost no one has gone down. Almost no one has come back. The few who have are either Court-licensed (and bound to silence) or, much more often, fugitives whose accounts no one believes.

docs.title: Travel and Flight
docs.text:
Flight technology exists on Aetherion. Gnomish turbines, gravitic regulators, and the engineering that moves the continent itself can be miniaturized into personal or industrial-scale craft. But personal flight is not a civilian culture. The tech is expensive, specialized, and historically locked to industrial, military, and elite use. Ordinary citizens travel overland.

  • Civilian: the League of Feathered Roads — halfling caravan guild — runs the continent's everyday transit infrastructure. Skybridges between cities, ropeways through canyons, overland caravans, river boats. Most Skyborn live and die on this network.
  • Elite / specialized: small private flying craft exist for Highcrown nobles, gnomish industrialists, and a handful of mercantile elites. Visible but rare.
  • Military: Aetherion's military fields airships. Most citizens have seen them but never been aboard. The Skyguard and Cloud Knights of Aurion operate these.
  • Illicit: smuggler gliders and rigged contraptions exist, mostly around Tether's End. Dangerous, unreliable, and a hanging offense if caught using one for descent.

docs.title: Descent — The Pit Gate
docs.text:
Descent to the surface is gated at three layers:

  1. Theological and legal taboo. Descent is heresy. The Inquisition actively suppresses descent culture; the public is raised with sincere Pit-fear from the children's rhyme on up.
  2. Physical danger of the descent zone. The airspace between Aetherion and the surface is hostile: falling debris, atmospheric instability around the Anchor's resonance, shard interference, and the ever-present dread of the Fallen Powers below.
  3. Infrastructural bottleneck at the Grand Anchor. Tether's End is the only stabilized, predictable route down. Lifts, chains, smuggler routes — established but tightly controlled.

Descent is therefore committal. Anyone who goes down knows it's hard to come back up quickly. Almost no one tries.

docs.title: Tether's End
docs.text:
The continent's sixth cultural pole — and the only one that is not a nation. A ramshackle town of timber and rope lashed to the cliffs of Aetherion's southern rim, beyond Tumblebrook's farmland, where the land runs out and falls away into open sky. It clings to the ledges around the Grand Anchor — the ancient structure the whole continent moors to — and around the Great Chain that drops from it into the clouds below. If Aurion is where Aetherion is ruled and Khalvar Hold is where it is held up, Tether's End is where the continent meets its edge: the one place a person can, with the right permits and the wrong sense, go down.

Tether's End, clinging to the southern rim — the Anchor above, the Chain falling into the gulf below.

docs.title: The shape of the town — the Clinging Town
docs.text:
Where Aurion climbs a plateau and a person's station is how high they live, and Khalvar Hold is cut downward and a dwarf's standing is how deep she's trusted to go, Tether's End hangs out and down off the edge of the continent. The land ends at Landfall, where the Tumblebrook road stops; below it the town spills down the cliff-face on rope-walks and timber galleries, out toward the Anchor and the open sky. The further out and down you live, the harder the life — and, in the town's own reckoning, the more nerve it takes. Outsiders see a heap of the desperate sorted by how far they've fallen. The town sees a community sorted by how much edge a person can stand.

Five zones, from solid ground to the gulf:

  • Landfall — the Tumblebrook road's end, the caravan-yard, the customs post, and the last inns on solid ground. As far in as most travelers ever come, and the one foot the town keeps on the continent.
  • The Roost-Walks — the town proper, lashed to the cliff-face: the homes, the market, the Watch-House, and the Anchor and Lantern, all strung together by rope-bridges and switchback timber stairs.
  • The Salt Wharf — the working heart, where the town meets the Anchor: the cargo-lifts, the loading-floors, the brokerage office, and the lift gangs who are the town's largest trade.
  • The Underledges — the lowest ledges and scaffolds, clinging just above open sky: the poorest homes, the boldest workers, and the place the Great Chain plunges past into cloud.
  • The Descent — below the Anchor: the platform, the shaft, and the way down. Forbidden without a Court permit, which almost no one ever has.

The Clinging Town: the road's end at the top, the zones cascading down the cliff, and the Chain falling away below.

docs.title: Getting around
docs.text:
Tether's End gates nothing. Where Aurion checks you at every ring and Khalvar at every band, here anyone may wander from Landfall out to the last Underledge — the town is too poor and too tangled to police its own coming and going, and too fond of its freedom to want to. It is the one place on the continent where you need no token to cross. The single exception is the one that counts: the Anchor's descent platform, watched by the Riftwatch's Anchor-Wardens. You go anywhere in Tether's End freely — except down.

The town is held together by rope, plank, net, and salvaged iron: bridges between ledges, stairs pinned to the cliff, cargo-nets slung beneath the lowest walks to catch what falls. The Salvage Syndicate's counterweighted lifts run goods up and down the cliff. The one land route in is the caravan-road down through Tumblebrook's southern farmland, run by the League of Feathered Roads.

docs.title: The Great Chain
docs.text:
The marvel of Tether's End is its Chain. From the underside of the Grand Anchor a colossal chain of black, un-rusting metal — links a house could pass through, etched with symbols no scholar can read — descends past the lowest ledges and down into the cloud-gulf, vanishing from sight. It is the literal tether the town is named for: Tether's End is where the tether meets the world.

And the Chain sings — a deep, felt tone that rises off it as the drifting continent pulls against its mooring. At Landfall it is a hum at the edge of hearing; out on the Underledges it is a thrum you feel in your teeth and the soles of your feet, and a local can tell how far out they stand with their eyes shut. The town reads its moods: "the Chain runs low" means calm and safe; "she's singing high" means strain, and weather, and worse. People touch it for luck before a journey and knot the names of the fallen into the rope-walks beside it. "The Chain holds" is how a Tether's-Ender says all is well.

The Grand Anchor and the Great Chain — the tether the continent moors to, falling away into the gulf.

docs.title: What Tether's End is known for
docs.text:
• The Anchor and the only way down. The Grand Anchor is the continent's mooring and the only stabilized route to the surface. Descent is heresy and a hanging offense without a Court permit; the permits are almost never granted; and yet the way exists, here and nowhere else. That single fact is what the whole town is built on and around.
  • The Salvage Syndicate. The town's smugglers' guild and largest employer — lift gangs, cargo routes the watch politely doesn't notice, and the brokerage that moves goods across the Court's legitimacy line. Officially they don't exist. Practically they're who you pay rent to.
  • The Riftwatch. The Court's watch over the Anchor and the town — honest about being compromised, which is its own kind of integrity. They guard the descent and look past most of the rest.
  • A place for the unplaceable. Tether's End draws the people the five nations have no slot for: exiles, smugglers, anchor-workers, failed pilgrims, debtors, and those whose origins fit nowhere else. No one here asks where you're from. It is the freest, and the least safe, town on the continent.

docs.title: Living here
docs.text:
• Speech: Common, everywhere and to everyone. The town has no formal register and no patience for one.
  • Faith: the Chain and luck. Tether's End is the one settlement on Aetherion with no resident god and no tended temple — only a dusty Court shrine at Landfall for departing pilgrims, and the genuine folk-devotion people pay to the Chain itself.
  • Manners: blunt, generous in small things, and incurious by custom — asking a stranger's past is the town's one real rudeness. A reputation here is built on whether your word holds and whether you keep your nerve on a rope-walk in a gulf-wind. Nothing else counts.

docs.title: See also
docs.text:
• Aetherion (the continent) — the floating world Tether's End hangs off the edge of.
  • Travel and Flight — how people move across the continent, and the gated descent to the surface.
  • Riftwatch · Salvage Syndicate — the watch and the smugglers.

docs.title: The Anchor and Lantern
docs.text:
The town's tavern, and the heart of its public life — a broad, low-beamed common-room slung across one of the widest ledges of the Roost-Walks, where Tether's End drinks, deals, hires, mourns, and decides things. The name is the town in two words: the thing it hangs from, and the only light it makes for itself.

The Anchor and Lantern — the town's tavern, and the heart of its public life.

docs.title: The house
docs.text: A warm, crowded, swaying room of weathered driftwood and salvaged timber, with a great brass lantern over the bar that is never allowed to go out — the town's working superstition holds that while the Lantern burns, the Chain holds. Nets, floats, and the rope-tokens of the dead hang from the beams; the Great Chain's thrum comes up through the floor. The fare is gull-stew, hard bread, up-chain ale, and the rough cane-spirit the lift-and-run crews drink before dangerous work and call bottom-cup. A back wall of six chairs sits empty until the Salvage Syndicate's Boss wants them.

docs.title: The keeper — Doral Pitch
docs.text: A heavy, unhurried man who has kept the Anchor and Lantern longer than anyone can quite remember, kept the great Lantern lit the whole while, and kept every confidence spoken in the room — a tavern-keeper at the edge of the world who talks does not keep his tavern long. He likes a customer more the longer they keep coming back. If you want to know who in Tether's End to ask about a thing, Doral knows who's drinking tonight.

docs.title: Living here
docs.text: The Anchor and Lantern is where a newcomer becomes a regular and a regular becomes trusted. Where Doral seats you — at the bar, near the Lantern, or (rarely, and only by invitation) at the back — is the plainest measure there is of where you stand in the town.

docs.title: See also
docs.text:
• Tether's End — the town.
  • The Roost-Walks — the band the house hangs in.

docs.title: Landfall
docs.text:
The topmost part of Tether's End, and the one foot the town keeps on solid ground. Where the caravan-road down through Tumblebrook's southern farmland finally runs out of continent, there is a broad shelf of true rock — the last earth before the cliffs begin — and on it stand the caravan-yard, the customs post, a small shrine, and the last inns that rest on something that won't sway in a gulf-wind. Everything below Landfall hangs off the cliff. For most travelers and pilgrims, Landfall is as far out as they ever come — and the moment the world stops being solid.

Landfall — the caravan-road's end, and the last solid ground before the town begins to hang.

docs.title: What's here
docs.text:
• The caravan-yard. The League of Feathered Roads' terminus: corrals, a weigh-house, a posting-board, and the warehouses where goods from up the road are sorted before they go down into the town. Nearly everything that comes to Tether's End by land crosses this yard.
  • The customs post. A stone gatehouse with a permit-desk and a Court flag, where manifests are stamped and the prohibition on descent is, in theory, enforced. The Riftwatch staff it.
  • The shrine. A small, dusty shrine to the Twelve — the only temple in a town with no resident god — kept for pilgrims about to attempt the descent. The candle-seller beside it does better trade than the offering-box.
  • The last inns. A few real inns on solid rock, where a traveler who wants to see the famous Anchor and leave again can sleep without learning what a rope-walk is like in a storm. As respectable as Tether's End gets.

docs.title: Living here
docs.text: Landfall is the membrane between the lawful continent and the edge. It is where you are asked your business — the one place in town where anyone asks at all. How far down past it you're willing to go is the first thing the town learns about you, and most of what it cares to know.

docs.title: See also
docs.text:
• Tether's End — the town this is the threshold of.
  • The Roost-Walks — the town proper, just below.

docs.title: The Roost-Walks
docs.text:
The town proper — where most of Tether's End lives. Below Landfall's solid shelf the cliff-face begins, and the Roost-Walks are the swaying middle of the town pinned to it: a three-dimensional warren of rope-bridges, switchback timber stairs, and house-galleries bolted onto the rock and strung between every ledge that will hold a beam. From a distance it looks like a colony of cliff-birds' nests — hence the name. It is loud, close, salt-damp, and never quite still; the whole quarter moves a little in the wind, and a newcomer either stops noticing by the second week or doesn't last.

The Roost-Walks — homes and market-ledges lashed to the cliff, strung together by rope-bridge.

docs.title: What's here
docs.text:
• The Netmarket. The town's market, strung across a wide ledge and the bridges around it: fish and gull-eggs, grain and cloth from up the road, salvage-goods of doubtful origin, rope and tar and lamp-oil — the necessities of a town that makes almost nothing and imports almost everything. Cargo-nets are slung beneath it to catch what's dropped.
  • The Watch-House. The Riftwatch's seat: the Captain's office, the duty-board, a couple of cells, and the room where new watchers are sworn in.
  • The homes. Galleries of small dwellings worked into the rock and hung off it. A home deeper down the cliff is cheaper and more dangerous; one up near Landfall is dearer and duller. People move up and down as their fortunes and their nerve change.
  • The chain-wright's loft. The workshop of the wright who keeps the town's rope, cable, and beam sound — the single most quietly important job in Tether's End, since everyone's footing depends on it.

docs.title: Living here
docs.text: The Roost-Walks have no fixed map: a hard storm snaps bridges and strands ledges until they're re-rigged, and a bad winter takes a walk or two and whoever was on them. Locals navigate by the Chain's song — you always know which way is down-chain — and by a learned sense of which planks to trust. To move easily on the Roost-Walks in a gulf-wind is to be, unmistakably, a Tether's-Ender.

docs.title: See also
docs.text:
• Tether's End — the town.
  • Landfall above · the Salt Wharf below.

docs.title: The Salt Wharf
docs.text:
The working heart of Tether's End: the broad cargo-ledges where the town meets the Grand Anchor, and where the Salvage Syndicate's lifts haul everything that moves up or down the cliff. If the Roost-Walks are where the town lives, the Salt Wharf is where it works — and where it makes the only money it makes. It is the busiest, best-lit, most orderly place in town, because carelessness here drops cargo and crews into the gulf, and no one can afford either.

The Salt Wharf — the lift-heads and cargo-floors where the town meets the Anchor.

docs.title: What's here
docs.text:
• The lift-heads. Great timber-and-iron gantries pinned to the cliff, where the town's counterweighted cargo-lifts terminate. The lift gangs who run them are the largest single trade in Tether's End, and the weekly lift-schedule posted here is the Wharf's clock.
  • The cargo-floor. The loading-ledge where crates come off the up-chain caravans and onto the lifts: leathers, ores, salt-cured fish, lamp-oil, and a good deal that nobody names. Loud with the lift gangs' hand-signals and the cant of crews who have worked together for years.
  • The brokerage office. The Syndicate's room above the Wharf, where buyers meet sellers — the most polished space in a town with no polish.
  • The wall of names. A plank wall by the lift-heads where the names of lift-hands and runners who fell are knotted into the rigging, one cord per name. The closest thing Tether's End has to a memorial; the crews touch it before a dangerous run.

docs.title: Living here
docs.text: The Salt Wharf is where you go to find work, move goods, or hire the kind of help the Court doesn't license. Respect here is earned by usefulness and by not getting anyone killed — the only two virtues the Wharf recognizes, and the only two it needs.

docs.title: See also
docs.text:
• Tether's End — the town.
  • The Roost-Walks above · the Underledges below.

docs.title: The Underledges
docs.text:
The lowest part of Tether's End: the last ledges and scaffolds clinging to the cliff before the rock gives out and there is only open sky, the Great Chain, and the gulf. The poorest homes in town are here, and so are its boldest people — on the Underledges the two are often the same. This is as far down-chain as a person can live and still be in the world, and the Chain's song here is not a hum but a bone-deep thrum you feel through the soles of your feet. Outsiders find the Underledges terrifying. The town finds them honest: no one on the last ledge is pretending to be anything.

The Underledges — the lowest cling-homes, and the Great Chain plunging past into cloud.

docs.title: What's here
docs.text:
• The cling-homes. Single rooms on the smallest viable ledges and slung in the netting beneath the lowest walks — the cheapest rent in town and the dearest in nerve. Home to the long-term exiles, the debtors, the failed pilgrims, and the lift-and-run crews who want to be near the work.
  • The staging-ledges. Where the Salvage Syndicate's crews rig and launch their work, out of sight of Landfall and the customs post.
  • The Chain's plunge. The Underledges are where you can stand closest to the Great Chain as it falls past the cliff into cloud — the most-visited and least-spoken-of spot in town. The dying are carried out here to hear it; the desperate come to decide things; fallers' names are knotted along the nearest rail.

docs.title: Living here
docs.text: To live on the Underledges is to have run out of up-chain — or to have chosen the edge on purpose. The town respects the people of the last ledge in a way it rarely says aloud, because they hold their nerve where no one else will live, with the gulf a single misstep away and the Chain singing in their bones.

docs.title: See also
docs.text:
• Tether's End — the town.
  • The Salt Wharf above.

docs.title: The War of the Three Shards
docs.text:
3rd century A.S. — roughly 2,700 years ago.

In a single season, three burning stars fell:

  • The Blue Flame to Sylvaris.
  • The Red Ember to Ankarum.
  • The Golden Crown to Highcrown.

Each was unmistakably significant — far larger and stranger than ordinary Skyfall fragments, each visibly tied to the symbolism of one of the Twelve.

The three nations went to war over them. After a decade of bloodshed, the season's three relics had become three different kinds of tragedy:

  • Blue Flame: shattered. Fragments lost.
  • Red Ember: vanished in an underground collapse. The Ember Faith awaits its bearer's return.
  • Golden Crown: sealed by Highcrown, "by the will of Majesty," never displayed again.

After the war, the Majestal Court declared open shard-war heretical. No nation has fought another nation for shards since.

"If three shards fall again, the skies themselves will burn."

The War is remembered annually at the Court's High Holiday of Mourned Heaven — three days of black banners in every capital, with the Ember Faith holding its longest vigil of the year.

docs.title: Ankarum (Dwarf)
docs.text:
Capital: Khalvar Hold. In the old dwarven tongue, the name means the Mountain-Keeper's Hold.

Ankarum is the dwarven nation, set into the great mountains of Aetherion's northern reaches. Fortress culture: vaulted halls cut into the stone, runic engineering, mining, formal manners, long memories. The dwarves are widely regarded as the load-bearing people of the continent — both literally and as a matter of national temperament.

docs.title: What Ankarum is known for
docs.text:
• The Keelwrights. The dwarven engineering guild seated in Khalvar Hold, whose runic engineering and stabilizing chambers keep Aetherion structurally whole. Without them, the continent shears apart within months. Every licensed rune-smith on Aetherion holds a Keelwright license. See Keelwrights of Ankarum.
  • Mountain craft. Stone-cutting, smelting, rune-smithing, structural engineering. Ankarum supplies precision goods to every other nation.
  • The war-host. Ankarum's standing army is small but professional. Dwarven Skyguard chapters are stationed at the eastern garrisons; the war-host's senior officers are drawn from veterans of decades of border patrolling and the rare skyquake-response deployment.
  • The Ember Faith. A dwarven splinter sect that worships the lost Red Ember — the shard that fell to Ankarum centuries ago and vanished during an underground collapse — and awaits its bearer's return. Tolerated within Ankarum as a fringe variant rather than full heresy.

docs.title: Society at a glance
docs.text:
• Public language: Dwarven (the old register) is used in formal contexts; Common is everyday.
  • Naming: patronymic. Brynnirsson (son of Brynnir), Halvarsdottir (daughter of Halvar). Place-names tend toward consonant-heavy compounds — Khalvar, Korunheim, Brynhalla.
  • Houses and guilds: dwarven society is organized around guild affiliation as much as bloodline. A senior Keelwright outranks most nobles in practice.
  • Aesthetic: stone, iron, deep colors, lamp-light. Beards braided with metal rings indicating guild rank.

docs.title: Other dwarven institutions
docs.text:
• The Black Anvil. A heretic cult that believes the Ankarum mountain interior hides ancient caverns — the lost forges where, they say, the gods made the first shards. Widely dismissed as cranks, occasionally prosecuted as heretics when they're loud enough.
  • The Greywatch. A mountain-edge hermitage outside Khalvar Hold, associated with the goddess Melancholy. Pilgrims travel there in times of grief.

docs.title: See also
docs.text: • Khalvar Hold — the capital.

docs.title: Khalvar Hold
docs.text:
Capital of Ankarum, and the structural heart of the whole continent. In the old dwarven tongue the name means "the Mountain-Keeper's Hold." It is cut downward into the great mountains of Aetherion's northern reaches — a city of vaulted halls, deep forges, and the great Stabilizing Halls whose runic engines keep the continent from shearing apart. If Aurion is where Aetherion is ruled, Khalvar Hold is where it is held up.

Khalvar Hold, cut downward into the mountain — cold at the gate, warm in the deep.

docs.title: The shape of the Hold — the Descending City
docs.text:
Where Aurion climbs a plateau and a person's station is how high they live, Khalvar Hold is cut the other way — downward, into the mountain — and a dwarf's standing is how deep she is trusted to go. The surface belongs to outsiders and trade; the deep belongs to the work that matters, and you earn your way down by skill and a guild's license, not by birth. Ask a dwarf "how deep do you go?" and you are asking what she is.

Five bands, surface to deep:

  • The Gatefort — the great gates in the mountain's face, the muster yard of the war-host, the Skyguard garrison, and the caravanserai where the continent's traders and travelers are housed. As far as most outsiders ever come.
  • The Hearthhalls — the living-city: the vaulted markets, the guild-streets, the homes, the alehouses, and the great chapter-house of the Ember Faith. Warm, crowded, lamp-lit, and proud.
  • The Anvil Tiers — the working deep: the foundries and forges, the workshops of the rune-smiths, and the hall of the First Anvil of the Keelwrights, where the thousand-year-old Master Rune-Book is kept.
  • The Sanctums — the temple-deep: the deep shrines, the War Sanctum where warriors are blessed before battle, and the Hall of Stilled Hammers where the Hold mourns its dead.
  • The Deep — the Stabilizing Halls themselves, crewed by the Keelwrights who live underground for months at a stretch, and below them the unmapped stone. Few dwarves are ever cleared this far; fewer still go willingly.

The Descending City: the five bands, the Kingstair threading down, and the mourning-roads on the outer face.

docs.title: Getting around
docs.text:
Each band is sealed from the one below by a ward-lock — a guild-warded gate-chamber. You go deeper by clearance: a guild token, a work-order, a sworn errand. The deepest locks answer to Keelwright rank alone. The one road open to any cleared dwarf is the Kingstair, the great switchback stair that drops through every band; ore and stone ride counterweighted lifts, but the living climb back on foot. (There are no sedan-chairs in Khalvar. A dwarf walks.)

A ward-lock on the Kingstair — you go deeper only when you're cleared to.

The Hold's south gate, the Bryndur gate, opens outward onto the mountain's face — the head of the Ember Faith's three-day Pilgrim Trail down the Bryndur ridge, and the path to the Greywatch hermitage.

docs.title: The Deepwarmth
docs.text: The marvel of Khalvar is the opposite of Aurion's. Aurion's blessing is its water, born at the summit and falling cool through every ring. Khalvar's blessing is its warmth, born in the deep and rising. The Gatefort is cold mountain air; descend, and the stone grows warm and begins to hum — a low, felt note rising from far below. The dwarves call it the Deepwarmth, and they revere it as the sign that the mountain holds and the work continues. "The deep runs warm" is how a dwarf says all is well. You can tell how deep you are with your eyes shut, by the warmth on your skin and the hum in your teeth.

docs.title: What Khalvar Hold is known for
docs.text:
• The Keelwrights. The dwarven engineering guild seated in the Anvil Tiers, whose runic engineering and Stabilizing Halls keep Aetherion structurally whole. Every licensed rune-smith on the continent holds a Keelwright license. The First Anvil — the guild's senior — outranks most nobles in practice.
  • The forges. Stone-cutting, smelting, rune-smithing, and the war-host's armories. Khalvar supplies precision goods and weapons to every nation.
  • The Ember Faith. The dwarven mourning-sect that keeps the Records of Loss and the long Pilgrim Trail, and awaits the return of the lost Red Ember. Its great chapter-house in the Hearthhalls keeps a Watching Light that has not gone out in living memory.
  • The mourning-roads. The Greywatch hermitage on the mountain's outer face, sacred to the goddess Melancholy, and the Pilgrim Trail down the Bryndur ridge to the old cave-in shrine. Dwarves walk the Trail at least once in a life; many walk it in grief.

docs.title: Living here
docs.text:
• Speech: Dwarven (the old register) in formal and guild contexts; Common everywhere else. How deep you're cleared marks your standing more than how you talk.
  • Faith: the Deepwarmth and the work. The forge-bells mark the shifts; the Ember Faith keeps the year's great Vigil; warriors pray to Maelstrom before any contest.
  • Manners: formal, blunt, and slow to trust — but a trust once given is not lightly withdrawn. A dwarf who calls you cleared has decided something about you.

docs.title: See also
docs.text:
• Ankarum — the nation.
  • The bands: The Gatefort · The Hearthhalls · The Anvil Tiers · The Sanctums.
  • Places: The Greywatch.

docs.title: The Greywatch
docs.text:
A hermitage on the mountain's outer face above Khalvar Hold's Gatefort, sacred to Melancholy, the goddess of grief. A grey stone cloister clinging to cold rock, open to the wind and the long view down the Bryndur ridge — reached by the Hold's south postern, and the high anchor of the mourning-roads. Pilgrims have come here in grief for two thousand years.

The Greywatch: Melancholy's cold cloister on the mountain's outer face.

docs.title: The cloister
docs.text: A modest house of grey stone — cells, a refectory, a cold chapel, and a walled garden of hardy mountain plants. No Deepwarmth reaches it: the Greywatch is cold by design, and quiet enough to hear the wind and your own heart. Mourners are housed without question and without fee. The staff ask nothing and offer presence rather than comfort — which is the Greywatch's whole teaching, and Melancholy's: yes, this hurts; yes, it should; no, you do not have to be done with it.

docs.title: Melancholy's Voice
docs.text: The hermitage is the home of Wundra Greyhall, the gaunt itinerant mourner who is the goddess's public Voice, traveling constantly to wherever loss is happening across the five nations and returning to the Greywatch between her journeys. To meet her on the road in your grief is, the dwarves say, a hard mercy.

docs.title: The mourning-roads
docs.text:
From the Hold's Bryndur gate, the Pilgrim Trail runs three days down the ridge to the old cave-in shrine — the path the Ember Faith walks in mourning, and which most dwarves walk at least once in a life. The Greywatch is its high anchor.

The Pilgrim Trail down the Bryndur ridge, the Greywatch on the rock above.

docs.title: See also
docs.text: • Khalvar Hold — the Hold the hermitage watches over.

docs.title: The Anvil Tiers
docs.text:
The working deep of Khalvar Hold — the band below the Hearthhalls where the Hold makes things. Here the Deepwarmth becomes real heat: the great foundries, the war-host's forges, and the workshops of the rune-smiths. The air is hot and metal-tasting, loud with hammer and bellows, the stone-hum overlaid with a forge-roar. You are cleared into the Anvil Tiers only with cause — a guild token, a work-order, a sworn errand.

The Anvil Tiers: the forge-deep, where the Deepwarmth becomes real fire.

docs.title: What's in the works
docs.text:
• The foundry-floors. Smelting-halls and casting-floors where Khalvar's ore becomes its precision goods and its weapons, and — walled apart under the war-host — the armory-works.
  • The rune-shops. The licensed rune-smiths' workshops, where structural runes and shard-work are inscribed to Keelwright standards. Unlicensed rune-work is forbidden and prosecuted by the guild itself.
  • The First Anvil's hall. The seat of the Keelwright guild council and the chamber of the First Anvil, the guild's senior — where the thousand-year-old Master Rune-Book is kept, and from which every licensed rune-smith's copy is signed.
  • The lift-heads. The great counterweighted ore-lifts that move stone and metal between the foundries and the deeper bands.

docs.title: The guild's deep
docs.text: The Anvil Tiers are the Keelwrights' ground, and the Keelwrights are the real power in Khalvar. A senior Wright outranks a merchant prince; the First Anvil outranks most nobles. To work here is to belong to the guild that holds the continent together — and the guild guards its standards, its licenses, and its rune-craft with a jealousy that has the force of law.

docs.title: See also
docs.text:
• Khalvar Hold — the city overall.
  • The Hearthhalls — the living-city above · The Sanctums — the temple-deep below.

docs.title: The Gatefort
docs.text:
The surface threshold of Khalvar Hold — the topmost band, where the mountain's face is cut into a fortress of gates and the world outside is let in only this far. The Gatefort is cold and wind-scoured, the one part of the Hold that sees open sky; snow lies here while the deep runs warm. For most travelers, the Gatefort is Khalvar Hold — they are housed, fed, and traded with here, and never see another band.

The Gatefort: the iron gate-valves and the muster yard, where the world is let in only this far.

docs.title: What stands at the gate
docs.text:
• The Gate-valves. The Hold's enormous iron-faced outer doors, each as tall as a tenement, closed by counterweight against any assault.
  • The muster yard. The war-host's parade and drill ground, where Ankarum's army and its dwarven Skyguard chapters form up.
  • The Skyguard garrison & moorings. The standing-army barracks and the masts where Skyguard airships moor.
  • The Outroad. The visitors' quarter just inside the gates — the caravanserai, the customs-house, the moneychangers and licensed factors, and the warehouses where foreign goods are inspected. The one cosmopolitan corner of a closed city, loud with five languages.

The Gatefort's south postern, the Bryndur gate, opens outward onto the mountain's face — the head of the Ember Faith's Pilgrim Trail and the path to the Greywatch.

docs.title: Passing inward
docs.text: To go deeper than the Gatefort, an outsider needs a sponsor, a writ, or a guild token; goods are inspected at the customs ward-lock, and the unlicensed are turned politely back. The Hold keeps its strangers at the door — not from rudeness, but from long habit. The deep is for the work, and the work is dwarven.

docs.title: See also
docs.text:
• Khalvar Hold — the city overall.
  • The Hearthhalls — the living-city just below.

docs.title: The Hearthhalls
docs.text:
The living-city of Khalvar Hold — the great band of vaulted caverns just below the Gatefort where most of the Hold lives. Warm, crowded, lamp-lit, and proud: the Deepwarmth is comfortable here and the stone-hum is a background murmur you stop hearing after a day. This is the Hold's broad middle, and the first band an outsider reaches once cleared past customs.

The Hearthhalls: the great market-cavern, lamp-amber against grey stone.

docs.title: What's in the halls
docs.text:
• The Great Market (the Hearthfloor). The central market-cavern — stone-stalls, guild-shops, and food-halls, where the daily commerce of the Hold is done. Smith-work and precision goods, deep-grown mushroom-fare and barley-ale, and luxuries brought up through the Gatefort. The Hall-Caller cries the guild-postings and the Court's decrees from the Hearthfloor steps at the shift-bells.
  • The house-galleries. Tiers of carved family dwellings, each door-lintel marked with clan and guild.
  • The guild-streets. The halls of the lesser guilds — masons, victuallers, lamp-wrights, and the trade-houses.
  • The Ember chapter-house. The great house of the Ember Faith, the most prominent faith-building of the band, whose Watching Light has not gone out in living memory.

docs.title: The warmth of shared endurance
docs.text: The Hearthhalls belong to the Ember Faith — but dwarven warmth is not festival-warmth; it is the warmth of endurance shared. The Faith is the neighborhood's social fabric: its Watch-Mothers sit with the bereaved, its Records of Loss remember the dead, and its annual Vigil is the Hold's most reliably-attended event. To live in the Hearthhalls is to be known — your clan, your guild, your griefs — and, in the dwarven way, looked after.

docs.title: See also
docs.text:
• Khalvar Hold — the city overall.
  • The Gatefort — the surface above · The Anvil Tiers — the working deep below.

docs.title: The Sanctums
docs.text:
The temple-deep of Khalvar Hold — the band below the Anvil Tiers, where the Hold keeps its sacred places and its dead. The Deepwarmth here is blood-warm to the palm and the stone-hum is felt in the chest; the dwarves hold the warmth of the Sanctums to be the nearest a living dwarf comes to the gods' own hearth. Few outsiders are ever cleared this deep, and none without a sworn dwarven escort.

The Sanctums: the War Sanctum's blessing-dais beneath the war-host's banners.

docs.title: What's in the sacred deep
docs.text:
• The deep shrines. Carved god-niches and chapels worked into the warm stone, where the Twelve are honored — though the band's true devotions are dwarven: Maelstrom for the warriors, Melancholy for the grieving, and the wordless reverence of the Deepwarmth itself.
  • The War Sanctum. A fortified vault where the warriors of the war-host are blessed before battle. Maelstrom's holiest place in the Hold; warriors across the continent pray toward it.
  • The Hall of Stilled Hammers. The Hold's mourning-deep — a vast, quiet chamber where the dead are laid in wall-niches and the great clans keep their ancestral vaults, and where the hammers of dwarves who died at their craft are hung, stilled. The Ember Faith's most solemn rites are held here.

docs.title: See also
docs.text:
• Khalvar Hold — the city overall.
  • The Anvil Tiers — the working deep above.

docs.title: Glymswick (Gnome)
docs.text:
Capital: Cogbarrow.

If Ankarum keeps Aetherion whole, Glymswick keeps it moving. The gnomes' wind-harnessers, arcane turbines, and gravitic regulators control the continent's drift. Their crowning project is the Crowngear — the great mechanism beneath Cogbarrow that lets Aetherion be subtly steered against the prevailing winds.

The Gearwright's Consortium runs propulsion, the Crowngear, and drift mechanics via patents, licensing, and the polite ruin of competitors.

docs.title: What Glymswick is known for
docs.text:
• The Crowngear. The continent's drift-control mechanism, three levels underground in Cogbarrow's central works. Without it, Aetherion goes where the winds put it.
  • Engineering culture. Gnomish artifice — clockwork, alchemy, propulsion, optics, resonance — is the continent's standard. Cogbarrow workshops train the engineers every nation eventually borrows.
  • The patent system. Continent-wide intellectual property regimes are a Glymswick invention. The Consortium owns most of the patents that matter.
  • The Convention. Annual gathering of Engineer-Wrights. Cogbarrow is busiest in early spring.

docs.title: Society at a glance
docs.text:
• Public language: Common, with a heavy load of technical and craft loanwords.
  • Naming: craft-suffix. Steamspoke, Twogear, Pendulum, Glasswork. Surnames mark the original family trade, though most gnomes work outside it.
  • Aesthetic: brass, glass, lamp-light, polished wood. Long aprons, goggles pushed up on foreheads, ink-stained sleeves.
  • Geography: Cogbarrow sits over the Crowngear's enormous underground chamber; the city is built on tiered platforms over the central works. Smaller Glymswick towns spread along the gentler ridgelines.

docs.title: See also
docs.text: • Cogbarrow — the capital.

docs.title: Cogbarrow
docs.text:
Capital of Glymswick, the gnomes' great city, seated over the Crowngear on the continent's eastern reach. If Aurion is where Aetherion is ruled and Khalvar Hold is where it is held up, Cogbarrow is where it is steered: the city that sits atop the vast mechanism by which the gnomes angle the whole drifting continent against the wind. It is the loudest, brightest, most inventive place on Aetherion — and the only city where a stranger's worth is read off a number stamped on a brass plate.

Cogbarrow, ringed around the Crowngear — the inner city turning slowly over the great mechanism that steers the continent.

docs.title: The shape of the city — the Turning City
docs.text:
Where Aurion climbs a plateau and a person's station is how high they live, and Khalvar Hold is cut downward and a dwarf's standing is how deep she's trusted to go, Cogbarrow turns inward — and a gnome's standing is how far in toward the Gear their work is licensed to sit. The city is built as a great set of concentric rings around the central Works that drops to the Crowngear. The outer rings are fixed ground, and anyone's to walk. The inner rings are the turning ones — they revolve, slowly and ceaselessly, over the buried Gear — and to set your workbench on them you must earn the right by patent, license, and proven invention.

This makes Cogbarrow the one city sorted by paper. "What's your number?" is the question here, and the number is the one stamped on the brass plate every licensed gnome wears: it says how far in you're cleared to work, and so it says who you are. You don't climb your way up or earn your way down in Cogbarrow. You file your way in.

Five rings, outer to inner:

  • The Rimworks — the fixed-ground edge: the unlicensed tinkerers' sheds, the immigrant quarters, and the great Convention fairground. Anyone may work here; no plate required. Where every gnomish career, and most gnomish inventions, begin.
  • The Manufactories — the working city: the licensed workshops, the foundries, the markets, and the homes of most of Cogbarrow, with the transfer-yards along its inner edge where the turning city is boarded.
  • The Brass Rings — the credentialed heart, where the Turning begins: the master-workshops and guild-halls of the Engineer-Wrights, and the Patent Office that gates the whole inner city.
  • The Gearhead — the innermost, fastest-turning ring: the seat of the Gearwright's Consortium and its Senior Council, crown of the Works.
  • The Underworks — below the Works-shaft: the Crowngear itself, tended by the deep-licensed and seen by almost no one.

The Turning City: fixed rings without, turning rings within, the Crowngear at the heart — and the Last Gate Mausoleum standing apart, the one great thing that does not turn.

docs.title: Getting around
docs.text:
Cogbarrow gates with paperwork. Where Aurion checks you at a guarded gate on every ring and Khalvar at every band, here the gate is the brass plate stamped with your patent-number and license-grade. The outer rings are open to all; to board the turning rings you show your plate at the transfer-thresholds, and a plate that doesn't clear the grade simply isn't waved across. The true gate of the city is the Patent Office, where a number is granted, raised, or revoked.

You board the inner rings at transfer-bridges — platforms where a fixed gallery meets a moving one and you step across at the moment they align, the way a gnome child learns to step onto a slow carousel. Conveyor-walks and counterweighted lifts carry the rest, all of them keeping time to the Turn. A Cogbarrow gnome reads the hour, the shift, and the date off the alignment of the turning rings against the fixed city, without ever glancing at a watch — and can tell an outsider at once by the half-beat they're always behind.

docs.title: The Turning
docs.text:
Cogbarrow's marvel is that its heart turns. The inner city revolves — slowly, ponderously, ceaselessly — once a day over the buried Crowngear, slow enough to live on and vast enough that the rings nearest the center sweep visibly past the fixed ground at the rim. With it comes a deep, slow pulse, one beat as each great tooth of the Gear engages, felt in the floor and the breastbone.
