The gnomes revere it: it is the sign that the Gear runs, that the continent is steered, that the work goes on. "The Gear turns" is how a Cogbarrow gnome says all is well — and in living memory it has never once stopped.

The Turning — the inner rings revolving over the Crowngear, the pulse of the Gear felt through the whole city.

docs.title: What Cogbarrow is known for
docs.text:
• The Crowngear. The continent's drift-control mechanism, deep beneath the city, by which the gnomes angle Aetherion's heading against the prevailing winds. Without it, the continent goes where the wind puts it. Only the highest engineers know its full workings.
  • The Turning City. The only city on Aetherion whose heart revolves — a feat of gnomish engineering unmatched anywhere, and the source of the city's whole sense of time.
  • The patent system. Continent-wide intellectual-property law is a Glymswick invention, and the Patent Office is its temple. The Consortium owns most of the patents that matter, and the brass plate is the gnome's name, rank, and permit in one.
  • The Last Gate Mausoleum. The continent's great burying-place, drawing pilgrims and grieving heirs from all five nations — and the one great structure in Cogbarrow that deliberately does not turn.
  • The Convention. The Consortium's annual gathering of Engineer-Wrights, held on the Rimworks fairground in early spring, when Cogbarrow is busiest and an unknown tinkerer can change their life in three days.

docs.title: Living here
docs.text:
• Speech: Common, with a heavy load of technical and craft loanwords; the gnomish craft-cant is what you learn if you mean to rise. Naming is craft-suffix — Steamspoke, Twogear, Pendulum, Glasswork — marking a family's original trade.
  • Aesthetic: brass, glass, lamp-light, polished wood. Long aprons, goggles pushed up on foreheads, ink-stained sleeves, and the brass plate at every belt and door.
  • Manners: Cogbarrow respects proven work above blood, charm, or coin. Shop-talk is the real conversation, the plate is read before the face, and the surest way to earn a gnome's regard is to follow the cant and build something that holds.

docs.title: See also
docs.text:
• Glymswick — the nation Cogbarrow steers.
  • Aetherion (the continent) — the floating world the Crowngear keeps moving.
  • The Rimworks · The Manufactories · The Brass Rings · The Gearhead — the rings of the city.
  • The Last Gate Mausoleum · The Patent Office · The Idle Wheel — the landmarks.

docs.title: The Brass Rings
docs.text:
The credentialed heart of Cogbarrow, and where the Turning begins. Inward of the fixed Manufactories, the city lifts onto the great revolving platforms of the Brass Rings — the master-workshops, the guild-halls, and the Patent Office that gates the whole inner city, all of them riding the slow rotation of the Gear. To set your bench here is to have arrived: it means an Engineer-Wright's credentials, or a license-grade high enough to be trusted with work that matters — and it means working, eating, and sleeping in slow motion over the Crowngear.

The Brass Rings — the master-workshops and the Wrightwalk, riding the Turn over the Gear.

docs.title: What's here
docs.text:
• The master-workshops. The Engineer-Wrights' own shops — large, immaculate, and individually famous, each a small kingdom of patents. Work done here is Consortium-sanctioned and Consortium-owned; nothing leaves without a licensing letter.
  • The guild-halls. Where the credentialed conduct the Consortium's middle business — license hearings, patent disputes, journeyman examinations — slow, formal, and conducted to the beat of the Turn.
  • The Patent Office. At the threshold where the Manufactories meet the Brass Rings stands the Patent Office, the bureaucratic gate of Cogbarrow, where a number is granted, raised, contested, or revoked.
  • The Wrightwalk. The great circumferential gallery where the credentialed promenade and deal — Cogbarrow's answer to a noble court's corridors, except everyone is an engineer and the gossip is about patents.

docs.title: Living here
docs.text: To live on the Brass Rings is to live on the Turn: in slow, ceaseless motion over the Gear, within a world of patents, gradings, and the quiet, vicious politics of an engineering nation. It is the most coveted address in Cogbarrow for everyone who is not yet on it — and a place where standing, once won, must be defended in paper for the rest of a career.

docs.title: See also
docs.text:
• Cogbarrow — the city.
  • The Manufactories outward · The Gearhead inward.
  • The Patent Office — the gate at this ring's threshold.

docs.title: The Gearhead
docs.text:
The innermost, fastest-turning ring of Cogbarrow and the crown of the Works: the seat of the Gearwright's Consortium, where the Senior Council sits, the Crowngear is governed, and the most consequential decisions on the continent's drift are made. It is cleared only to the deep-licensed — the Senior Council, their senior staff, and the Calibration engineers who tend the Gear — and it revolves visibly faster than the rings around it, so that to stand at its rail is to watch the whole city wheel slowly past beneath you.

The Gearhead — the Consortium's seat, turning fastest of all over the head of the Works.

docs.title: What's here
docs.text:
• The Senior Council chamber. Where the Consortium's twelve-member council sets policy, allocates research funding, and decides who is licensed. Werrik Steamspoke — master of propulsion engineering and the Consortium's most famously driven leader — chairs it, deliberating directly over the head of the Works he governs.
  • The Calibration galleries. Where the Crowngear's adjustments are ordered, logged, and dispatched down to the engineers below; the quarterly Calibration Reports are drafted here.
  • The Consortium's high offices. The administrative crown of the syndicate — the chartering, the licensing-of-last-resort, the patent appeals the Patent Office cannot settle.

docs.title: A closed place
docs.text: Few who are not Consortium will ever set foot in the Gearhead; for most of Cogbarrow it is the famous, unreachable center, glimpsed turning overhead and known by its decisions rather than its rooms. The drive-god Motive is the patron most associated with the Consortium's relentless ambition, and the Gearhead is the heart of that drive — the place where gnomish striving is turned to the steering of a continent.

docs.title: See also
docs.text:
• Cogbarrow — the city.
  • The Brass Rings — the ring just outward.
  • Motive — the god of ambition, patron of the Consortium.

docs.title: The Manufactories
docs.text:
The working body of Cogbarrow: the broad ring of licensed fixed-ground city between the unlicensed Rimworks and the turning Brass Rings. This is where most gnomes live and most real work is done — the foundries, the licensed workshops, the markets, the guild-streets, the homes, and the transfer-yards where the city is boarded onto the Turn. It is the long middle of a gnomish working life: licensed, productive, respectable, and not yet anywhere near the Gear.

The Manufactories — licensed workshops and the transfer-yards where the city steps onto the Turn.

docs.title: What's here
docs.text:
• The licensed workshops. Thousands of journeyman shops that hold a real Consortium license: clockwork, optics, alchemy, propulsion-parts, instrument-making — the standard of the continent, built here and shipped everywhere. The brass plate by a workshop's door is its rank, and the whole street can read it.
  • The foundries. Cogbarrow's metalworking heart: brass, bronze, fine steel, and the precision casting the rest of the continent cannot match.
  • The transfer-yards. The marked platforms along the inner edge where the fixed city meets the turning Brass Rings, and where a plate is shown and a step is taken at the alignment. Cogbarrow's great social threshold — where you watch who gets waved across to the Turn and who is sent back.
  • The telegraph exchange. Cogbarrow's clockwork telegraph network, tapping coded messages ring to ring faster than a runner can cross; the exchange where the lines meet is one of the best-informed rooms in the city.

A transfer-yard — stepping across onto the turning ring at the alignment, plate in hand.

docs.title: Living here
docs.text: The Manufactories are the everyday Cogbarrow — shifts changing on the Turn, markets keeping clockwork hours, a brass plate on every door, and the steady competence of a whole nation that builds for a living. It is comfortable, busy, and respectable, and most who live here will spend their lives here, within sight of a Turn they never quite board.

docs.title: See also
docs.text:
• Cogbarrow — the city.
  • The Rimworks outward · The Brass Rings inward.
  • The Idle Wheel — the engineers' alehouse on the inner edge.

docs.title: The Patent Office
docs.text:
The true gate of Cogbarrow. Where Aurion has guarded tier-gates and Khalvar has ward-locks, Cogbarrow has a bureau — and the Patent Office is it: the place where a number is granted, raised, contested, or revoked, and therefore the place that decides who is anyone in the city. It sits at the threshold of the Brass Rings, where the fixed Manufactories meet the turning heart, so that to win your patent is, almost literally, to be admitted to the Turn. No one fights their way into Cogbarrow's center. They file.

The Patent Office — the Registry, the Grading Hall, and the plate-press that stamps a gnome's name and rank in brass.

docs.title: What's here
docs.text:
• The Registry. Where every patent, license, and brass plate on the continent is recorded, graded, and renewed — the most complete record of who-built-what anywhere, and the difference between an Engineer-Wright and a Rimworks tinkerer.
  • The Grading Hall. Where license-trials are held — the examinations that raise a number and clear a gnome further in. Public, formal, and merciless; a failed grading is a year's wait.
  • The Appeals benches. Where patents are contested and licenses fought over — the slow civil war of an engineering nation, conducted entirely in paper.
  • The plate-stamp. The brass press that strikes the licensing plate every gnome wears — patent-number, licensed user, expiration. To be stamped up is to be reborn a ring inward; to be stamped down is Cogbarrow's bloodless exile.

docs.title: Living with it
docs.text: The Patent Office is famously slow, scrupulously fair on paper, and quietly ruthless in practice — a misfiled form can end a career without a voice ever being raised. To a Cogbarrow gnome it is simply how the world is ordered: by record, by grade, and by the number on the plate. To an outsider it is the strangest gate on the continent — one you cannot climb, bribe, or fight, but only satisfy.

docs.title: See also
docs.text:
• Cogbarrow — the city this office gates.
  • The Brass Rings — the turning heart the Office admits you to.

docs.title: The Rimworks
docs.text:
The outermost ring of Cogbarrow: the wide band of fixed ground at the city's edge, where the turning heart gives way to solid earth and a person needs no plate to stand. This is where the unlicensed work — apprentices, immigrants, washed-out academy hands, and the legion of speculative tinkerers chasing the patent that will carry them inward. It is the loudest, cheapest, smokiest, and most inventive part of the city, and the place where nearly every gnomish career — and most gnomish inventions — begin.

The Rimworks — the unlicensed edge, where the next idea is hammered out on a rented bench.

docs.title: What's here
docs.text:
• The tinkerers' sheds. Rows of rented benches, shared lathes, and lean-to workshops where the unpatented chase their idea: a better regulator, a cleaner lens, a turbine that finally holds. Most fail; a few file a patent and are gone inward within the year.
  • The Convention fairground. A great open ground, quiet most of the year and roaring in early spring, when the Consortium's annual Convention draws every Engineer-Wright and aspiring journeyman to Cogbarrow — three days of demonstrations, trials, and deals, and the one window when an unknown can put a device in front of someone who matters.
  • The immigrant quarters. The most mixed-folk neighborhood in any of the five capitals — human, half-elf, halfling, and dwarf hands drawn to Cogbarrow for work the Consortium licenses without much caring who holds the tools, so long as the paper is in order.

The Convention — three days each spring when an unknown tinkerer can change their life.

docs.title: Living here
docs.text: The Rimworks is where Cogbarrow lets anyone try. There is no plate to show and no ring to clear; there is only whether what you build works, and whether you can get it in front of the right eyes before your savings run out. It is the freest part of the most documented city on the continent — and the hardest place to stay, if the inward door never opens.

docs.title: See also
docs.text:
• Cogbarrow — the city.
  • The Manufactories — the licensed ring just inward.

docs.title: The Last Gate Mausoleum
docs.text:
The continent's great burying-place, and the holy seat of Mortis, god of endings: a vast funerary complex set apart on its own quiet terrace at the edge of Cogbarrow, where the finest gnomish craft is turned entirely to the work of death. Pilgrims and grieving heirs travel from all five nations to lay their notable dead here, in clockwork ossuaries and precision-cut sarcophagi tended by automaton mourners — gnomish invention bent to grief, and unmatched anywhere on Aetherion.

It is also, by deliberate design, the one great structure in Cogbarrow that does not turn. Where the whole licensed city revolves over the Gear, the Mausoleum stands on fixed rock, still and silent, its great clock-tower the one unturning timepiece against which the city keeps its hours. The gnomes find this exactly right: the city of restless invention turns forever; the house of endings holds still.

The Last Gate Mausoleum — the still place, and the clock that does not turn.

docs.title: What's here
docs.text:
• The Last Gate. The threshold-arch the Mausoleum is named for — the gate the dead pass and the living turn back from. Funerary processions from across the continent end here.
  • The clock-tower. The great still pendulum-clock at the heart of the complex — the one fixed clock of Cogbarrow, whose slow, even beat keeps not the city's hours but, mourners say, the count of endings. Its honorific surname, Pendulum, is borne by the family that has kept it for generations.
  • The crypts. Descending galleries of the honored dead, ordered not by wealth but by the craft of their memorial — the gnomish reckoning that a life is measured by the work it leaves behind.
  • The automaton mourners. Clockwork figures that keep the vigils no living mourner can sustain — pacing the galleries, tending the lamps, bowing at the hours. Some have kept their stations for centuries.

docs.title: Mortis here
docs.text: Mortis's public face at the Last Gate is Nirrik Pendulum, the long-tenured gnomish overseer who runs the Mausoleum and tends its rites. Stern, formal, and kind, the clergy here ease the dying in their last hours and honor the dead without flinching from what death is — Mortis's edict is to speak the truth about endings, and his house keeps it.

docs.title: See also
docs.text:
• Cogbarrow — the city.
  • Mortis — the god of endings, whose seat this is.

docs.title: The Idle Wheel
docs.text:
Cogbarrow's best-loved engineers' alehouse, on the inner edge of the Manufactories, with a long gallery-window that looks straight out over the transfer-yards onto the slowly turning Brass Rings — so the regulars drink within sight of the Turn most of them can't yet board. The name is an engineer's joke and an engineer's ache: an idle wheel is a gear that turns with the train but does no work of its own, which is what every journeyman in the room is on his own time, and what half of them privately fear they'll always be.

The Idle Wheel — the off-shift engineers' house, its ceiling flywheel turning in time with the city.

docs.title: The house
docs.text:
A warm, brass-lit, clattering room: polished wood and glass, gear-wheels and dead patents nailed to the beams as trophies, and a great slow flywheel set in the ceiling that turns in time with the city's Turn — wound, the keeper insists, from the Gear's own beat. The ale is real, the tea is taken seriously, and the house specialty is bench-coffee, thick and bitter, the fuel of every all-night build in the Manufactories. Shop-talk is the only conversation, and a stranger who can follow the cant is halfway to welcome before they buy a round.

The keeper is Etterly Sprung, a dry, sharp-eyed gnome who reads every newcomer's brass plate before they reach the bar and seats them accordingly — a small, constant performance of Cogbarrow's whole social order. She keeps the flywheel wound, the bench-coffee hot, and every confidence in the room.

docs.title: See also
docs.text:
• Cogbarrow — the city.
  • The Manufactories — the ring the house sits on.

docs.title: Highcrown (Human)
docs.text:
Capital: Aurion.

The political and religious center of Aetherion. The Majestal Court interprets the will of the Twelve from the Majestal Palace at Aurion's heart; the Crown Assembly jockeys beneath it; the Skyward Inquisition hunts heresy; the Order of the Pure Flame guards shards and pursues unauthorized bearers as crusaders.

To live in Highcrown is to live at the heart of the Twelve's reign. The capital, Aurion, hosts the great festivals of the calendar; the streets are kept by the Skyguard and patrolled by the Inquisition; the Crown's courts dispense judgment.

docs.title: What Highcrown is known for
docs.text:
• The Court. The Majestal Court is the most powerful institution on Aetherion. Sermons, festivals, the calendar, censorship, judicial appeal — all flow from Aurion.
  • The Inquisition and the Pure Flame. The Court's enforcement arms. Heavy presence in Aurion, lighter but real in every other capital.
  • The Crown Assembly. Fifty-three noble Houses organized as a deliberative body. Alliances and marriages drive Aurion politics.
  • The Skyguard. Highcrown's standing army. Airships, conventional forces, ceremonial guard. The most-photographed military in the five nations and the most politically constrained.
  • The Bank. The Bank of Aurion mints the faith coin and holds most noble debts. Quietly central to everything.

docs.title: Society at a glance
docs.text:
• Public language: Common, in the formal Aurion register.
  • Naming: dynastic. House names matter; given names are inheritable. Hadrian of House Auremont, Brienna of House Caltherine.
  • Aesthetic: marble, gold leaf, banners. Long ceremonial robes for senior offices. A perpetual undercurrent of scripture quoted at conversational volume.
  • Geography: Aurion sits on the continent's highest plateau, ringed by terraced estates. Roads radiate outward to the other capitals.

docs.title: See also
docs.text: • Aurion — the capital.

docs.title: Aurion
docs.text:
Capital of Highcrown, and the capital of the whole flying continent — the seat of the Majestal Court, the home of the gods-in-residence, and the destination every pilgrim on Aetherion hopes to climb at least once.

If you are reading this, you probably live here. This is your city.

Aurion, capital of Highcrown — and of the continent.

One city on a whole floating world: Aurion crowns the high heart of the continent, with forests, farmlands, mountains, and the other realms spread below.

docs.title: The shape of the city
docs.text:
Aurion is built up the sides of the highest plateau on the continent, in great rings that climb to the summit. The higher you go, the grander it gets — and the harder it is to get there. Five rings, from the top down:

  • The Crown — the summit. The Majestal Palace, the great temples of the Twelve, and the Mirror of the Twelve, the shining reservoir that crowns the city. Where the Court sits and the gods keep their seats.
  • The Coronet — the noble ring. The terraced estates and water-gardens of the fifty-three Houses, the Crown Assembly's hall, and the Solnaris Opera House. Marble, gold leaf, and old family pride.
  • The mid-quarter (formally the Burgage Tiers) — the working heart of the city. The Bank of Aurion, the guild-streets, the craftsfolk and merchants, the markets. Most of the city's real business happens here.
  • The Low Quarters — the base of the plateau, ringed by the canal and its docks. Tenements, brewers, fishmongers, watermen. Poor, crowded, and the friendliest part of the city if it likes you.
  • The Spillways — the drains and sluices below the Low Quarters, where the canal pours off the plateau's edge. Nobody much goes down there.

The rings of Aurion, with the Sacred Way climbing from the docks to the Crown.

docs.title: The rings, seen
docs.text:
The Crown: the Mirror of the Twelve and the great temples at the summit.

The Coronet: the terraced estates and water-gardens of the noble Houses.

The mid-quarter: the guild-streets and markets, the golden summit far above.

The Low Quarters: the canal and its docks at the foot of the city.

docs.title: Getting around
docs.text:
Each ring is walled off from the one above by a gate-stair, kept by the Skyguard. Passing upward takes a reason — the right dress, the right errand, or a gate-token stamped for the tier you're climbing to. Most folk live and work their whole lives without setting foot above the mid-quarter.

The one road open to everyone is the Sacred Way, the great processional avenue that switchbacks all the way from the pilgrim-gates at the base to the temples at the Crown. Cargo and water are hauled up the tiers on gnomish water-lifts and barge-locks; nobles ride enclosed chair-funiculars; everyone else takes the stairs.

The Sacred Way — the one road open to all, climbing from the base to the Crown.

A tier-gate: the guarded stair between one ring and the next. You climb only with reason to.

docs.title: The Descent — the water of the gods
docs.text:
Aurion's marvel is its water. At the summit, the Mirror of the Twelve is fed by engines that draw moisture from the high winds the continent rides, and from the Mirror the water cascades down through every ring of the city — through the nobles' water-gardens, into the public fountains and canals, down at last to the Low Quarters and the docks. The faithful call it the Descent and drink it as a blessing: the gods' gift, flowing down to all.

You can tell how high up the city you are with your eyes shut. The Crown is cool and misted and clean; the Low Quarters smell of canal-smoke, brewing mash, and fish.

docs.title: The pilgrim city
docs.text:
As the seat of the pantheon, Aurion is where the faithful of all five nations come. The Calendar of the Twelve rules the city's year, and its great festivals bring pilgrim tides that pack the inns, fill the alms-houses, and crowd the Sacred Way with relic-sellers, preachers, and the devout. For a few weeks at a stretch the whole city swells, and then empties again.

A festival night: the whole city strung with lanterns, the temples floodlit above, sky-lanterns rising.

docs.title: What Aurion is known for
docs.text:
• The Court. The Majestal Court is the most powerful institution on the continent — sermons, festivals, the calendar, the courts, all flow from the Crown.
  • The gods-in-residence. More of the Twelve keep their seats in Aurion than anywhere else. Majesty presides from the Palace at the Crown. Mercy keeps the great Aurelium Hospice, whose doors open onto the Low Quarters so the poor and the dying can reach her without climbing. Muse composes for the Solnaris Opera House on the Coronet.
  • The Inquisition and the Pure Flame. The Court's enforcement arms. The Skyward Inquisition's fortress, the Pyre Bastion, looks down over the working tiers — a reminder, never far from view, of who keeps the faith.
  • The Bank of Aurion. Mints the faith coin and holds most of the noble Houses' debts. Quietly behind everything.

docs.title: Living here
docs.text:
• Speech: Common, in the formal Aurion register. How you talk marks which tier you're from.
  • Faith: the calendar runs the day. Bells mark the hours; rest-day empties the workshops; scripture is quoted at conversational volume.
  • Rank: in Aurion, your station is something you can see — it's how high up the city you live, and climbing it is the work of a lifetime.

docs.title: See also
docs.text:
• Highcrown — the nation.
  • The rings: The Crown · The Coronet · The Mid-Quarter · The Low Quarters.
  • Places: The Solnaris Opera House · Aurelium Hospice · The Wayshrine.

docs.title: Aurelium Hospice
docs.text:
The kindest place in Aurion — Mercy's great hospice, formally House Aurelium, called simply "the Hospice" by locals and "Mother Lethaiyn's House" by the patients who keep coming back. It sits at the bottom of the mid-quarter with its main doors opening onto the Low Quarters, so the poor and the dying can reach it without climbing or showing a gate-token. It is the one great institution of the upper city that reaches down.

Mother Lethaiyn — matron of the Hospice, in whom the city sees Mercy's own compassion.

docs.title: The house
docs.text: A long colonnade of bright, clean wards around a willow court with a fountain of clear Descent-water. Pale blue and white everywhere; the open-hand sigil of Mercy over every door; doves in the willow at dawn. It smells of soap and herbs instead of canal-smoke, and people lower their voices here out of respect rather than fear.

docs.title: Its people and reach
docs.text:
• Mother Lethaiyn — the elven matron in whom the city sees Mercy's own compassion, and the soul of the house. She still walks the wards most evenings and will sit with a dying stranger for hours.
  • The ward-staff — lay-healers and novices who admit the hurt, assign the beds, and ask few questions.

The Hospice anchors a wide network of nurses, lay-healers, and traveling missionaries that reaches every major city on the continent — Mercy's compassion made into infrastructure.

docs.title: See also
docs.text:
• Aurion — the city overall.
  • The Mid-Quarter — the ring the Hospice sits at the foot of.

docs.title: The Mid-Quarter
docs.text:
The working heart of Aurion — the middle rings of the plateau, where the merchants, guilds, and craftsfolk do the business that keeps the city fed and funded. Formally the Burgage Tiers; almost nobody calls it that. It spans a gradient: prosperous at the top, crowded at the bottom.

The guild-streets and markets — the city's working heart, the golden summit far above.

docs.title: What's here
docs.text:
• The First District (upper mid-quarter). The wealthy mercantile core — the Bank of Aurion, the great guild-halls, the money-changers, the chartered trading houses. Clean cobbles and broad frontages.
  • The guild-streets (the middle). The working trades, street by street: the brass-founders, the paper-and-ink streets, the jewelers' run, the cloth halls, the clockmakers. Each guild keeps a hall and a chapel to its patron among the Twelve.
  • The lower seam (toward the Low Quarters). Where the clean cobbles give way to crowding — small shops, lodgings over workrooms, the trades that never made guild-rank.
  • The Aurelium Hospice. Mercy's great hospice sits at the very bottom of the ring, its doors opening onto the Low Quarters so the poor and the dying can reach it without climbing.

The public fountains and trade-canals here are fed by the Descent flowing down from the summit; the guilds compete fiercely for water-rights for their workrooms.

docs.title: Living here
docs.text: The mid-quarter knows it is not noble and never will be, and has made a virtue of competence. People judge you by your trade and your word. It is friendlier than the Coronet and warier than the Low Quarters — a place that respects anyone who pays on time.

docs.title: See also
docs.text:
• Aurion — the city overall.
  • The Coronet — the noble ring above; The Low Quarters — the base below.
  • Aurelium Hospice — Mercy's house, at the foot of the ring.

docs.title: The Coronet
docs.text:
The noble ring of Aurion — the terraced estates that climb between the merchants' mid-quarter and the gods' summit, home to the fifty-three Houses of the Crown Assembly. Above the money and below the gods, the Coronet is where Highcrown's marriages, inheritances, tariffs, and alliances are arranged. Marble, gold leaf, and old family pride, terraced so that even among the nobility the higher House looks down on the lower.

The noble ring: terraced estates and private water-gardens, the lower city beyond.

docs.title: What's known of it
docs.text:
• The Crown Assembly's hall. Where the fifty-three Houses sit, adjudicate noble disputes, and set the continent's tariffs. Its great winter convocation, the Annual Concord, is the social event of the year.
  • The water-gardens. On the Coronet the Descent becomes ornament and status — private cascades, reflecting pools, and cold-baths, each House's water a measure of its standing.
  • The Solnaris Opera House. The premier stage of Highcrown, endowed by House Solnaris, where Muse's operas premiere. The front door to noble society.
  • The Pyre Bastion. The fortress of the Skyward Inquisition, set on a spur beneath the ring and looking down over the working tiers — a reminder, never out of view, of who keeps the faith.

docs.title: Reaching it
docs.text: The gate up from the mid-quarter is the hardest social threshold in the city. A commoner passes only with a noble errand and the right token; most never do. To be invited onto the Coronet — to a ball, a premiere, an audience — is itself a mark of having arrived.

docs.title: See also
docs.text:
• Aurion — the city overall.
  • The Crown — the summit above; The Mid-Quarter — the working ring below.
  • The Solnaris Opera House — the heart of Coronet society.

docs.title: The Crown
docs.text:
The summit of Aurion — the topmost ring of the city, where the Court rules, the gods keep their seats, and the Mirror of the Twelve crowns the whole capital in shining water. It is the most sacred ground the Skyborn know, and the most closely kept: almost no commoner ever sets foot here. From the Crown the whole city falls away beneath you and the clouds are close.

The sacred summit: the Mirror of the Twelve, ringed by the great temples.

docs.title: What stands at the summit
docs.text:
• The Majestal Palace. Seat of the Majestal Court and the High Throne, where Majesty presides. Every doctrine, festival, and decree of the Twelve flows from these halls.
  • The temple-precinct of the Twelve. Twelve great temples ring the Mirror, one for each of the Twelve — their splendor a hierarchy in stone, from Majesty's gleaming dome to the quiet, neglected house of Madness. The high festivals are staged here, and the pilgrim tides that fill Aurion all climb toward this ring.
  • The Mirror of the Twelve. A vast, still reservoir of clear water at the very crest, the source of the Descent that flows down through every ring of the city. The faithful understand it as the gods' gift made visible.
  • The Pure Flame's chapterhouse. The seat of the Order of the Pure Flame, the Court's militant guardians of shards, who pursue unsanctioned relic-bearers across the continent.
  • The Skyhold. Aurion's airship moorings at the wind-open edge — the Skyguard's great garrison and the Cloud Knights' Cloud Lances, which fly only at the Throne's command.

docs.title: Reaching it
docs.text: The Crown sits behind the city's highest gates. For most Skyborn it is a silhouette seen from below their whole lives — glimpsed up close only during the great festivals, when the Sacred Way opens all the way to the summit and the faithful are permitted to climb.

docs.title: See also
docs.text:
• Aurion — the city overall.
  • The Coronet — the noble ring just below.

docs.title: The Low Quarters
docs.text:
The base of Aurion — the lowest inhabited ring, where the plateau levels into the canal basin and the working poor of the capital live and labor. Where the mid-quarter is proud of its competence and the Coronet of its blood, the Low Quarters is proud of its own: poor, crowded, smoke-stained, and fiercely loyal to the people who share its streets.

The Sallows and its docks at the foot of the city, the golden summit faint above.

docs.title: What's here
docs.text:
• The Sallows — the working canal that rings the base, the Descent's grey tail-water, and the spine of the district.
  • The Sallow Docks — where barge-locks lift cargo up the tiers; cranes, warehouses, watermen, and the morning shout at fourth bell.
  • The tenement rows — half-timbered buildings pressed shoulder to shoulder, slate roofs, and the rows of old cast-iron lanterns the Crown stopped paying to light a decade ago.
  • The markets — corner stalls and the dockside fish-market, where the faith coin is counted in coppers.

By the time the Descent reaches the Low Quarters it has served every ring above; the faithful still call it the gods' gift and still drink it.

docs.title: Faith and the people
docs.text:
Where the Crown belongs to Majesty, the Low Quarters belongs to Mirth — the warm, common-folk god, whose small lay-temples are the neighborhood's gathering places, weddings, and festival-nights. Mercy's Aurelium Hospice opens its doors here too, the one institution from above that reaches down without making the poor climb to it.

It is a tight, friendly, watchful place. People know each other's names and business; strangers stand out within the hour. The Low Quarters protects its own.

docs.title: See also
docs.text:
• Aurion — the city overall.
  • The Mid-Quarter — the working ring above.
  • Aurelium Hospice — Mercy's house, whose doors open here.

docs.title: The Solnaris Opera House
docs.text:
Highcrown's premier stage and the cultural heart of Aurion's noble ring — a gilded, tiered house endowed by and named for House Solnaris, where the operas of Muse premiere before they tour to Sylvaris. To be seen in a Solnaris box on a premiere night is to announce that you matter.

Maestra Cylleth Solnaris — the house's artistic head, and the publicly honored Voice of Muse.

docs.title: The house
docs.text: Five tiers of horseshoe galleries in white and gold beneath a ceiling painted with the nine arts of the Muse. The grand boxes below belong to the great Houses; the cheap upper galleries are open to anyone who can spare the coin — one of the few ways a commoner sets foot in the Coronet legitimately.

docs.title: Who you'll hear of
docs.text:
• Maestra Cylleth Solnaris — the house's artistic head and conductor of its great premieres, and the publicly honored Voice of Muse.
  • Intendant Domenec Halar — the impresario who runs the house: casting, bookings, and the endless politics of who sits in which box.
  • La Soreni — the reigning soprano, dazzling and indiscreet, the toast of the season.

docs.title: See also
docs.text:
• Aurion — the city overall.
  • The Coronet — the noble ring the house sits in.

docs.title: The Wayshrine
docs.text: Where the Sacred Way — the great processional avenue that climbs all five rings of Aurion — passes through the mid-quarter, it widens into a flagged plaza around a wayshrine of the Twelve: a place for footsore pilgrims to rest, light a taper, buy a blessing, and give alms before the long climb to the Crown.

docs.title: The plaza
docs.text: A tiered shrine of twelve niches, kept fresh with flowers and coin (Majesty's and Mercy's the best-tended; Madness's empty and unswept), a forest of guttering tapers, and a ring of stalls and mendicants. Incense, candle-smoke, festival-food, and the babble of a dozen nations' accents. On festival days the pilgrim tides pack it shoulder to shoulder.

docs.title: Faces of the Way
docs.text:
• Goodwife Tamsin Reedy — a licensed relic-seller with the best-placed stall, who sells blessed trinkets to pilgrims and cheerfully admits most of them are honest junk.
  • Brother Cobb — a gentle, half-deaf mendicant who tends the tapers and the alms-bowl, and has sat at this shrine so long the city flows past him unnoticed.

docs.title: See also
docs.text: • Aurion — the city overall, and its Sacred Way.

docs.title: Sylvaris (Elf)
docs.text:
Capital: Moonbough.

Luminous trees, suspended gardens, prophetic observatories, moon pools. The Sylvari Council of Boughs is a loose confederation of elven houses — ostensibly united, persistently divided. The Archivists of Moonbough maintain the continent's greatest library, with an unbroken record dating back generations before the Skyborn era.

Among the five nations, Sylvaris is the city of memory. Its capital, Moonbough, is a canopy city grown downward through living wood into the great descending Library — and a Sylvari's standing is not how high they live or how deep they dig but how far back they are trusted to read.

Elven longevity means the oldest elves remember centuries the rest of Aetherion only reads about. Most treat those memories as private, the way other peoples treat their grandparents' old letters.

docs.title: What Sylvaris is known for
docs.text:
• The Great Library. The Archivists' Library of Moonbough is the largest accumulated archive on the continent, descending through the canopy city's roots into chambers older than the Skyborn era.
  • The conservatories. Sylvari scholarly training produces most of Aetherion's senior historians, artists, and scribes.
  • Long memory. Sylvari oral tradition holds details about prior centuries that no other nation's records preserve.
  • The Council of Boughs. Confederation rather than monarchy. Decisions move slowly. Outsiders find the politics opaque.

docs.title: Society at a glance
docs.text:
• Public language: Sylvari Elvish in formal contexts; Common in trade.
  • Naming: hyphenated lineage. Saerith-wen-Maeloria, Iolyth-wen-Aerinwë. The wen particle marks maternal descent.
  • Aesthetic: woven living wood, suspended walkways, moon-glass, soft luminescence. Clothing in greens, silvers, and the occasional deep indigo.
  • Geography: Moonbough is a canopy city — its halls suspended in a forest of luminous trees with stacks descending into the deep root system. Other Sylvari settlements scatter across forested mountain valleys.

docs.title: See also
docs.text: • Moonbough — the capital (the Remembering City).

docs.title: Moonbough
docs.text:
Capital of Sylvaris, grown in the great luminous forest of the continent's western reach. If Aurion is where Aetherion is ruled, Khalvar Hold is where it is held up, Cogbarrow is where it is steered, and Tether's End is where it lets go, Moonbough is where it is remembered — the canopy city of the rememberers, where a person's standing is not how high they climb or how deep they dig but how far back they are trusted to read. It is the quietest, slowest, oldest-feeling place on Aetherion, and the only city where a stranger's worth is measured in memory.

Moonbough — the Remembering City, its living halls holding light from the bright canopy down into the old root-dark.

docs.title: The shape of the city — the Remembering City
docs.text:
Where Aurion climbs a plateau and a person's station is how high they live, Khalvar Hold is cut downward and a dwarf's standing is how deep she's trusted to go, and Cogbarrow turns inward and a gnome's standing is how far in their work is licensed to sit, Moonbough grows downward in time — and a Sylvari's standing is how far back into the record they are trusted to read. The city is not built but grown: the bright moonlit canopy above, where the young and the public and the visitors live; down through the great living-wood halls of the seven Houses; and down further into the Great Library's stacks, which descend through the canopy city's roots into chambers older than anyone can date.

The honor here is not the height and not the deep for its own sake — it is the age of what you are permitted to remember. You do not climb your way up or buy your way in. You are raised — by scholarship and proven memory — to read deeper into the record. "How far back do you read?" is the question here, and the answer says who you are.

Four tiers descend, canopy to root:

  • The Moonlit Crown — the bright public canopy: arrival, the moon-pools, the great Observatory, the suspended gardens, the conservatory schools, and the canopy markets. Anyone may walk here; this is the open face of the city.
  • The Living Boughs — the working city: the great living-wood halls of the seven Houses, the homes, the craft-galleries, and the Great Trunk where the Council of Boughs sits.
  • The Reading Boughs — the credentialed heart: the Great Library's working stacks, the Archivists' halls, and the Threshold of Boughs that gates the descent. To read here you must hold a grade.
  • The Sealed Boughs — the restricted deep: the oldest and most fragile holdings, and the root-dark beneath them, walked only by the Library's most senior Stewards.

The Remembering City as a tree: the bright canopy above, the Library descending through the trunk, the oldest record in the deep roots.

docs.title: Getting around
docs.text:
Moonbough gates with the reading-grade. Where Aurion checks you at a guarded gate on every ring, Khalvar at every band, and Cogbarrow by the brass plate, here the gate is how deep into the Library you are cleared to read — earned through the Bough Ascent, the Archivists' annual ceremony that raises a scholar who presents worthy work to a deeper grade of stack-access. The canopy is open to all; the working stacks need a grade; the Sealed Boughs answer only to the Lady of the Library's own grant.

The city is grown, not laid: you climb spiral stairs grown into the great trunks, cross galleries woven of living branch, and ride slow counterweighted moon-glass lifts down through the dark toward the deep stacks. There are no grand staircases of cut stone. The living wood holds a soft light — bright silver-green in the sunlit canopy, deepening to a cool, still silver as you descend — and a Sylvari can tell how deep and how old a place is with her eyes shut, by the colour of the glow.

docs.title: The Moonglow
docs.text:
Moonbough's marvel is that the wood itself holds light — a soft luminescence the Sylvari neither kindle nor put out, brightest in the young canopy and deepening to cool silver in the old root-dark. With it the moon-pools keep still, true reflections, in which the trained read the turning of the seasons and the shape of a coming year. The elves revere it: it is the sign that the wood remembers — that the long record of Sylvaris holds unbroken, that nothing true has been lost. "The wood remembers" and "the boughs hold light" are how a Sylvari says all is well.

The canopy halls by their own light — the Moonglow that the city keeps its memory by.

docs.title: What Moonbough is known for
docs.text:
• The Great Library. The largest accumulated archive on the continent, descending through the canopy city's roots into chambers older than any record can date. Its authentication carries continent-wide legal weight; its scholarship is the standard the other nations measure themselves against.
  • The Council of Boughs. Confederation rather than monarchy — seven elder Houses, each holding one Bough, deciding by the consensus of all seven. Slow by design. Outsiders find the politics opaque; the Sylvari find that useful.
  • Long memory. Elven longevity means the oldest Sylvari remember centuries the rest of Aetherion only reads about, and the conservatories of Moonbough train most of the continent's senior historians, artists, and scribes.
  • The moon-pools and the Observatory. House Aelinmoor's still pools and high observatories are the continent's center of astronomy and of the old Sylvari prophetic tradition.

docs.title: Living here
docs.text:
• Speech: Sylvari Elvish in formal contexts; Common in trade. Long names are normal and truncate aggressively in daily use.
  • Naming: hyphenated lineage — Saerith-wen-Maeloria, Iolyth-wen-Aerinwë — the wen particle marking maternal descent, often with a House name (of House Silverleaf).
  • Aesthetic: woven living wood, suspended walkways, moon-glass, and soft silver luminescence; clothing in greens, silvers, and the occasional deep indigo.
  • Manners: Moonbough respects age and memory above blood, charm, or coin. The surest way to earn a Sylvari's regard is to show that you remember accurately, read deeply, and are not in a hurry.

docs.title: See also
docs.text:
• Sylvaris — the nation Moonbough is the capital of.
  • The Sylvari Council of Boughs · The Archivists of Moonbough — the two institutions that run the city.
  • The Moonlit Crown · The Living Boughs · The Reading Boughs · The Sealed Boughs — the tiers of the city.
  • The Great Trunk · The Observatory · The Hollow Bole — the landmarks.

docs.title: The Living Boughs
docs.text:
The great middle tier of Moonbough, below the bright Crown and above the descending Library: the working city, where most of Sylvaris actually lives. Here the canopy thickens into the broad living-wood halls of the seven elder Houses, the homes and craft-galleries and guild-walks, and — at the heart of it, in the oldest and broadest fork of all — the Great Trunk where the Council of Boughs sits. This is the city's body: less luminous than the Crown, less hushed than the Library, the place where Sylvari life is lived rather than shown or kept.

The Living Boughs — the House-halls and craft-galleries of the working city, the Great Trunk at its heart.

docs.title: What's here
docs.text:
• The seven House-halls. Each elder House holds a great living-wood hall grown over centuries into a span of the city's middle canopy. A House's standing is read in the age of its hall's wood — the oldest grafts, the deepest-rooted boughs — not its grandeur.
  • The Great Trunk. The Council of Boughs' living assembly chamber, suspended in the canopy above the city's roots, where the seven Houses confer and (when they agree) speak with one voice.
  • The craft-galleries. The studios of the long-lived Sylvari crafts — illumination, moon-glass, instrument-making, and the slow weaving of living wood into hall and walkway. A craftsman may spend a century on a single hall.
  • The homes. Most of Moonbough's elves live here, in halls grown for families and lineages and woven through with the soft Moonglow — rarely built, almost never finished, added to and inherited down the centuries.

docs.title: Living here
docs.text: The Living Boughs are Moonbough at its most ordinary and its most rooted. A House's worth here is its memory and its patience; a craftsman's worth is the years in their work. Nothing is in a hurry, and the oldest, plainest halls — their wood dark and deep-grown — carry more standing than the brightest new ones.

docs.title: See also
docs.text:
• Moonbough — the city.
  • The Moonlit Crown — the public canopy above.
  • The Reading Boughs — the Library, one tier down.
  • The Great Trunk — the Council's chamber at the heart of this tier.

docs.title: The Moonlit Crown
docs.text:
The topmost tier of Moonbough: the bright, sunlit, public canopy of the city, where the great luminous trees open to the sky and a person needs no reading-grade to walk. This is where visitors arrive, where the young of Sylvaris grow up, and where the open life of the city happens — the moon-pools and the great Observatory, the suspended gardens, the conservatory schools, and the canopy markets strung between the crowns of the trees. It is the one tier of Moonbough that feels like a city to a stranger: airy, luminous, and busy in the slow Sylvari way.

The Moonlit Crown — the open canopy, where the city meets the sky.

docs.title: What's here
docs.text:
• The Mooncrown landings. The arrival-place: the great branch-platforms where the canopy roads and the embassy-roosts of the other nations meet the open city, and where the Council receives envoys. An outsider's first sight of Moonbough, and the furthest in most ever come.
  • The moon-pools. Still, mirror-dark pools held in the forks of the oldest trees and kept clear by the ritualists of House Aelinmoor. The lesser pools are open to all; lovers, pilgrims, and the curious come to look into the calm heart of the city.
  • The conservatory schools. The open faculties of the great Sylvari training pipeline — music, history, languages, illumination, ritual — that produce most of Aetherion's senior scholars, artists, and historians. Their commencement is the brightest festival of the Moonbough year.
  • The suspended gardens and canopy markets. Woven walkways carrying gardens, tea-houses, and the slow open markets where the city trades moon-glass, illuminated work, and distilled cordials.

docs.title: Living here
docs.text: The Crown is the part of Moonbough that anyone may see. There is no grade to show and no stack to clear; there is only the slow, luminous life of the canopy, and the quiet understanding that the real city — the record, and the right to read it — lies below, and is earned, not bought.

docs.title: See also
docs.text:
• Moonbough — the city.
  • The Living Boughs — the working city, one tier down.
  • The Observatory · The Hollow Bole — the landmarks of the canopy.

docs.title: The Reading Boughs
docs.text:
The credentialed heart of Moonbough, where the city stops living and starts remembering: the working tier of the Great Library of Sylvaris, descending from the Living Boughs into the trunk and the first reaches of the roots. This is where a reading-grade first matters — the threshold the open city gives way to, and the furthest most credentialed scholars ever go. Below it the stacks keep descending, restricted by grade, toward the Sealed Boughs in the deep.

The Reading Boughs — the working stacks of the Great Library, descending into the silver dark.

docs.title: What's here
docs.text:
• The working stacks. The vast accessible body of the Library — the cataloged, cross-referenced holdings open to any scholar of grade: histories, genealogies, cosmologies, and the authenticated record of the continent. Most of the order's Archivists work here, and its apprentice-scribes pipeline up through it.
  • The Threshold of Boughs. The gate of the descent: the landing where the working stacks meet the deeper, restricted ones, and where a warden reads your cataloging-stylus against the day's grade-list. Above it, the open scholar's world; below it, the stacks you must be raised to enter.
  • The Archivists' halls. The order's working seat — the cataloging-rooms, the restoration and copying workshops, the translation-benches, and the Wardens' studies.
  • The conservatory's deeper schools. Where promising apprentice-scribes are trained toward full credential, the pipeline that the open Crown conservatories feed into.

docs.title: The reading-grade
docs.text:
Moonbough gates once, here, and gates with grade. You descend past the Threshold only if your stylus carries the reading-grade for the stacks below — granted by the Bough Ascent, the annual ceremony in which a scholar presents worthy original work to the Lady of the Library and, if it is judged worthy, is raised to a deeper grade of access. It cannot be bought. It is, by long Sylvari design, earned.

The Threshold of Boughs — the one gate of a city that gates with knowledge: show your grade, or read no deeper.

docs.title: See also
docs.text:
• Moonbough — the city.
  • The Living Boughs — the working city above.
  • The Sealed Boughs — the restricted archive in the deep.
  • The Archivists of Moonbough — the order that keeps the Library.

docs.title: The Moonbough Observatory
docs.text:
House Aelinmoor's seat and the continent's center of astronomy and of the old Sylvari prophetic tradition: a cluster of high open platforms and deep, mirror-still moon-pools in the crown of Moonbough, where the ritualists read the turning of the sky and the shape of the coming year. The public faculty — star-charts, calendars, and the season-readings that farmers and sailors across the continent quietly rely on — is open in the canopy; the deep prophetic pools, and the rites worked over them, belong to House Aelinmoor alone.

The Observatory — the high lenses and the still moon-pools, where Sylvaris reads the sky.

docs.title: What's here
docs.text:
• The high platforms. Open observation-decks in the topmost canopy, with the continent's finest moon-glass lenses and the oldest continuous astronomical records. The senior astronomers of every nation defer to Moonbough's instruments.
  • The moon-pools. Still, mirror-dark water in the deepest forks, kept clear by rite. Trained pool-watchers read the seasons and the turning year, and — in the old tradition — fragments of what is to come.
  • The prophetic tradition. Sylvaris has always half-trusted the strange knowing of its seers. The Observatory's readings are taken seriously by the Council, and consulted on matters where the written record runs out.

A pool-reading — the old prophetic tradition at work, the sky read in still water.

docs.title: See also
docs.text:
• Moonbough — the city.
  • The Moonlit Crown — the canopy tier the Observatory crowns.

docs.title: The Sealed Boughs
docs.text: The deepest reach of the Great Library: the restricted descending stacks below the working tier, walked by the institution's three most senior Stewards and the Lady of the Library alone. This is where the Library stops being a public good and becomes a vault — the oldest, rarest, and most fragile holdings on the continent, kept in the cool silver dark of the deep roots.

docs.title: What's known
docs.text:
• The deep stacks. The most ancient holdings the Library keeps — fragments whose age and origin no scholar can fix, disintegrating originals too fragile to handle, texts in languages no living tongue reads, and documents held under Court restriction.
  • The Stewards. Three positions, always elves over four hundred years old, always nominated by the Lady herself. They walk the deepest stacks daily and speak rarely; they are tolerated as sacred-keepers and quietly regarded as half out of their own time.
  • The Sealed Bough rule. Nothing leaves the Sealed Boughs. Nothing. The Stewards may transcribe what they find; they may not remove it. It is the strictest rule in the institution.

Few outside the Library's senior ranks will ever see the Sealed Boughs, and fewer still know what they hold. To most of Sylvaris they are simply the proof that some things are old and fragile enough to be worth keeping carefully — and that the deepest memory of the continent is held in trust, not displayed.

docs.title: See also
docs.text:
• Moonbough — the city.
  • The Reading Boughs — the working Library above.
  • The Archivists of Moonbough — the order that keeps the deep stacks.

docs.title: The Great Trunk
docs.text:
The living assembly chamber of the Sylvari Council of Boughs, grown into the oldest and broadest fork of the Living Boughs: a vast hall of woven living wood suspended in the canopy above the city's roots, where the seven elder Houses convene three times a year and, when they can agree, speak with one voice. If the Library is where Moonbough keeps its memory, the Great Trunk is where Moonbough makes its decisions — slowly, by the consensus of all seven Boughs, at a pace outsiders find exasperating and the Sylvari do not adjust.

The Great Trunk — seven seats, seven Houses, and the slow consensus of the Council of Boughs.

docs.title: The chamber
docs.text:
A single grown hall, seven great living seats in a ring around an open floor, with a moon-pool set at the center to catch the sky. Each House holds one Bough — one seat, one vote — and nothing is decided but by the consensus of all seven; a single Bough's objection holds the whole Council. Above and apart sits the seat of the Standing Advisor — the Lady of the Library — who addresses the Council at the start of each session and is heard without interruption, though she holds no vote.

The Council is in formal session less than a third of the year. Most of its real work happens for weeks beforehand, in private House conference; the formal sessions ratify what the Households have already worked out.

docs.title: Who holds it
docs.text:
• The Speaker-of-the-Boughs — a rotating five-year office, currently held by Maelorin-ion-Saelath of House Eldengrove. Externally, the Speaker is the Council to the other nations; internally, the Speaker convenes and represents but does not rule.
  • The Seven Boughs — Eldengrove, Sablefern, Silverleaf, Aelinmoor, Thaliel, Cael-Thain, and Dawnglen, each speaking through its Bough Speaker.
  • The Standing Advisor — the Lady of the Library, heard on all matters of history and cosmology.

docs.title: See also
docs.text:
• Moonbough — the city.
  • The Living Boughs — the tier the Great Trunk sits at the heart of.
  • The Sylvari Council of Boughs — the body that meets here.

docs.title: The Hollow Bole
docs.text:
The scribes' and students' wine-house of Moonbough: a warm, glowing room grown inside the hollow trunk of a great old tree at the canopy's inner edge, where the open life of the Crown gives way to the hush of the Library. It is where the un-raised and the just-raised drink — apprentice-scribes who haven't made grade, journeyman Archivists who just did, conservatory students, and the washed-out who never will.

The name is a scribe's joke and a scribe's ache: a hollow bole is a great tree gone empty at the core — still living, still leafing, but with nothing left at the center to read in its rings. Which is what every un-raised scholar in the room privately fears they are.

The Hollow Bole — the scribes' house, lit by the heart-spiral in the living wood.

docs.title: The house
docs.text: A snug, spiralling room inside the living trunk, lit entirely by the wood's own glow — no lamps, just the silver in the grain, brightest along a slow heart-spiral that winds up the inside of the bole and pulses faintly, like a held breath. Worn reading-desks double as drinking-tables; rejected illuminations and un-raised work are pinned to the walls as trophies and warnings. The drink is real moonwine and good leaf-tea, and the house specialty is nightbark — a bitter, blue-black scholar's cordial that keeps a reader awake and sharp through the long dark of a deep-stack night.

docs.title: The keeper — Joriniel
docs.text: The keeper is Joriniel, an old elf and former apprentice-scribe who presented to the Bough Ascent three times and was never raised, and who keeps the house where the others like him drink. He does not discuss it. He keeps the nightbark hot, the heart-spiral tended, and every confidence in the room — a publican who repeats what the scribes let slip does not keep his custom. He has a soft spot for the passed-over and the cast-out, and his back tables are theirs.

docs.title: See also
docs.text:
