  • The Reading Boughs — the Library the house sits at the edge of.

docs.title: Tumblebrook (Halfling)
docs.text:
Capital: Greenmere.

Greenmere is small by capital standards because Tumblebrook is sprawled: green plains, terraced farms, mist-fed pastures, orchards, windmills, river-island villages. Tumblebrook feeds Aetherion. Most of the grain, most of the dairy, most of the autumn fruit that ends up on Skyborn tables passes through halfling hands.

The Halfling Tithe League quietly manages food distribution. The League of Feathered Roads runs the caravan and skybridge networks that carry the produce to every other capital. Halflings are patronized as "simple folk who feed the realm," and the trade networks they actually operate are some of the most powerful pieces of infrastructure on the continent.

docs.title: What Tumblebrook is known for
docs.text:
• Food. The breadbasket. Every grain, fruit, and dairy market on Aetherion is downstream of a Tumblebrook supplier.
  • The Roads. The League of Feathered Roads runs the continent's everyday transit network: caravans, ropeways, river boats, skybridges. Most Skyborn live and die on this network.
  • Festivals. Tumblebrook festivals are the calendar's biggest seasonal events outside Aurion's high holidays. Mirth's Midwinter is a Tumblebrook holiday in everything but official sponsorship.
  • Pacifism. Tumblebrook has the smallest standing military of the five nations and is widely (and accurately) believed to want to keep it that way.

docs.title: Society at a glance
docs.text:
• Public language: Common, in a softer rural register.
  • Naming: descriptive. Place-names, plant-names, animal-names. Reedwood, Whitlock, Pendulum (the gnomish family-name pattern occasionally turns up in mixed-lineage halflings too).
  • Aesthetic: woven cloth, wood, river stone, copper, garden flowers in season. Wide-brimmed hats. Pipe-smoking is socially acceptable in a way it is not in Highcrown.
  • Geography: the Tumblebrook plains roll across most of central Aetherion, broken by slow rivers and occasional mist-fed hill ridges. Greenmere sits at the largest river confluence.

docs.title: See also
docs.text: • Greenmere — the capital, the Laden Town.

docs.title: Greenmere
docs.text:
Capital of Tumblebrook, set at the great river-confluence in the lowland heart of the continent. If Aurion is where Aetherion is ruled, Khalvar Hold is where it is held up, Cogbarrow is where it is steered, Moonbough is where it is remembered, and Tether's End is where it lets go, Greenmere is where it is fed — the small, snug, laden town of the people who feed the realm, where a person's standing is not how high they climb or how deep they dig but how full their table and how many they set a place for. It is the warmest, fullest, least guarded place on Aetherion, and the only city where a stranger's worth is measured in welcome.

Greenmere — the Laden Town, the round Granary rising over the Green, the morning Welling lying thick over the plains that feed the realm.

docs.title: The shape of the town — the Laden Town
docs.text:
Where Aurion climbs a plateau and a person's station is how high they live, Khalvar Hold is cut downward and a dwarf's standing is how deep she's trusted to go, Cogbarrow turns inward and a gnome's standing is how far in their work is licensed to sit, Moonbough grows downward in time and a Sylvari's standing is how far back they read, and Tether's End hangs off the edge and a person's standing is how far out they dare, Greenmere does the one thing none of the others does: it lies flat and gathered around a hearth. A halfling's standing here is how close to the full table they sit and how many they feed at it — and the surest way to be thought well of in Greenmere is to keep a good table and set a place for a stranger.

The town is small — startlingly so for a capital — because Tumblebrook is sprawled: the nation is its terraced farms, mist-fed pastures, orchards, windmills, and river-island villages, and Greenmere is only the place where it all comes together to be reckoned, stored, blessed, and sent on. It is laid out not in rings of rank but as a set of gathered circles around a center, the way a halfling household lays a table from the hearth outward:

  • The Greenmere Granary — the still, full center: the great reserve that fills tables across the continent, and the offices of the Halfling Tithe League that reckon the realm's harvest.
  • Goodbarrow Hall — the great festival estate of the Goodbarrow line, the seat of halfling civic life and the host of Tumblebrook's most-anticipated celebrations.
  • The Festival Green — the open shared board at the town's heart: the great green and its long trestle tables, the markets, and the inns where no stranger eats alone.
  • The Cartway — the open rim where the land opens out toward the roads: the caravan-yards, the river-wharf, and the great skybridge to Aurion.

The Laden Town as a laid table: the Granary at the hearth, the Green at the heart, the roads at the rim.

docs.title: Getting around
docs.text:
Greenmere gates with hospitality. Where Aurion checks you at a guarded gate on every ring, Khalvar at every band, Cogbarrow by the brass plate, and Moonbough by the reading-grade, here there is no checkpoint at all — every door opens to a guest, and a stranger is fed before they are asked their business. The town is flat, snug, and walkable: a half-day's amble end to end, by lanes of low turf-roofed houses with garden-flowers, footbridges over the river-braids, the great Green at the heart, and the Granary's round towers rising over all. You find your way by the smell of bread and the sound of the river, and a halfling will walk you there sooner than tell you the way.

What graduates, in Greenmere, is not your access but your welcome: a stranger eats at the open Green, a guest at a household's own board, a friend at the back table, and family at the Granary's confidence. The hospitality never stops — but where you are seated says, exactly and warmly, how far into the town's trust you have come.

docs.title: The Welling
docs.text:
Greenmere's marvel is the gentlest on the continent: each dawn a soft white mist wells up from the river-braids and the mist-fed hollows and lies over the impossibly green plains until the sun lifts it. The halflings read it as the breath of a land that provides — a thick still Welling promises a fat year; a thin or early-burning one is watched with quiet unease. The town's weather-wisdom, its planting-lore, and half its festival calendar are timed to the moods of the morning mist. "The Brook runs full," "the Welling lay thick this morning," and "the table's laid" are how a Tumblebrook halfling says all is well.

The Welling — the morning mist the halflings read the year by.

docs.title: What Greenmere is known for
docs.text:
• The Granary and the Tithe League. The great reserve at the town's heart, and the agricultural co-op that coordinates the continent's food. The seasonal calls held here set what the other nations eat; the Granary's stores are large enough that everyone knows, and no one likes to think, what it would mean if Greenmere ever closed them.
  • The festivals. Tumblebrook's festivals are the biggest seasonal events on the calendar outside Aurion's high holidays, and Goodbarrow Hall throws the grandest of them. Mirth's Midwinter is a Tumblebrook holiday in everything but official sponsorship.
  • The Roads. Greenmere is the hub of the League of Feathered Roads — the caravans, ropeways, river boats, and skybridges that carry the realm's everyday traffic. The great civilian skybridge to Aurion's Lower Quarter, the largest on the continent, springs from the Cartway here.
  • Welcome. Greenmere's hospitality is proverbial across Aetherion. "Fed like a Greenmere guest" is a saying in five nations.

docs.title: Living here
docs.text:
• Speech: Common, in a softer rural register; full of food-and-weather idiom and proverbs.
  • Naming: descriptive and pastoral — given names short and warm (Penn, Pip, Daisie, Hob, Otto, Pearl), surnames invoking land, crop, and weather (Underbrook, Threshfield, Reedwater, Goodbarrow, Applefen). Many halflings go by nicknames their whole lives; road-traders often carry a route-name (Penn of the South Road) that travels with them.
  • Aesthetic: woven cloth, wood, river stone, copper, and garden flowers in season; turfed roofs, wide-brimmed hats, and pipe-smoking taken at leisure.
  • Manners: Greenmere respects a full table and an open door above blood, charm, or coin. The surest way to earn a halfling's regard is to be generous, unhurried, and good to feed — and the surest way to lose it is to be too grand to sit down and eat.

docs.title: See also
docs.text:
• Tumblebrook — the nation Greenmere is the capital of.
  • The Halfling Tithe League · The League of Feathered Roads — the two institutions that run the town and feed the realm.
  • The Festival Green · Goodbarrow Hall · The Granary · The Cartway — the rings of the town.
  • The Long Table — the inn at the Green's edge where the road's news pools.

docs.title: Goodbarrow Hall
docs.text:
The great festival estate of the Goodbarrow line — the beloved halfling family who have hosted Tumblebrook's most-anticipated celebrations for generations — and the seat of civic life in Greenmere. It is the largest festival hall in town, set a little in from the Festival Green, its long banner-hung halls and lantern-gardens spilling onto the Green when the season turns.

Goodbarrow Hall at festival — the grandest table in Tumblebrook, laid for the whole nation.

docs.title: The house
docs.text:
A rambling, generous estate of turf-roofed halls, lantern-gardens, brewhouses, and a great timber feast-hall whose central table is said to have seated nine generations of guests without once being taken apart. The Goodbarrows do not overawe; they host. The Hall's whole architecture is built to gather people in and feed them, and a Goodbarrow celebration is the social event the rest of the continent measures its own by. Garden-flowers in season, copper and bunting out of it, woodsmoke and baking bread always.

Tumblebrook has no court and no throne; it has the Hall. The seasonal festivals double as the nation's great gatherings — betrothals are blessed here, disputes are softened over food before they ever reach a formal hearing, and the whole sprawled nation feels, for a few days a season, like one household.

docs.title: The Goodbarrows
docs.text:
The Goodbarrows are halfling nobility of the gentlest kind: landed, beloved, and famous for generosity rather than power, and married into half the great kin-lines of Tumblebrook. The current head of house, Pearl Goodbarrow, is the charismatic and tireless host who throws the season's grandest events and is, by common agreement, the best-loved halfling in the realm. The line's ancient matriarch, Granny Goodbarrow, keeps to her quarters and attends the family festivals quietly from the elder's chair — a sweet, very old halfling at the warm edge of every celebration.

The greatest of the Hall's celebrations is Mirth's Midwinter, the festival of joy at the turn of the year — a Tumblebrook holiday in everything but official sponsorship, and the brightest night on the continent's calendar outside Aurion's high holidays.

docs.title: See also
docs.text:
• Greenmere — the town the Hall is the heart of.
  • The Festival Green — the open board the Hall's celebrations spill onto.

docs.title: The Cartway
docs.text:
The open rim of Greenmere, where the snug town opens out toward the roads and the river runs on toward the wider continent — the caravan-yards, the river-wharf, the depot of the League of Feathered Roads, and the great skybridge to Aurion. If the Granary is the still center where the realm's food gathers in, the Cartway is the edge where it rides out, and where the road-folk return laden with the world.

The Cartway — the hub of the continent's roads, and the foot of the great Aurion skybridge.

docs.title: The place
docs.text:
A working sprawl, the busiest and least pretty quarter of an otherwise gentle town: the great caravan-yards where the Feathered Roads' wagons are loaded and turned around; the river-wharf where flat barges take grain downstream; the cooperage and the wainwrights' and riggers' sheds; the League's waystation-inn; and, springing from the cliff at the southern edge, the Greenmere–Aurion skybridge — the largest civilian bridge on the continent, League-built and four generations in one family's keeping.

The Cartway is the hub of the whole continental transit network. Six major routes radiate from here — the South Road, the Mountain Road, the Anchor Road, the Sylvari Trace, the Cogbarrow Loop, and the Greenmere–Aurion main — and the League's published schedule that governs them is the most-consulted civilian document on Aetherion. If you have ever traveled anywhere on the continent by caravan, barge, ropeway, or skybridge, your journey was very likely planned, in part, on the Cartway at Greenmere.

docs.title: See also
docs.text:
• Greenmere — the town this is the rim of.
  • The League of Feathered Roads — the guild whose depot this is.
  • The Greenmere Granary — the center whose grain rides out from here.

docs.title: The Festival Green
docs.text:
The open shared board at the heart of Greenmere — the great common green and the markets, inns, and trestle-tables ringing it, where the whole sprawled nation comes to eat, trade, and celebrate together, and where no stranger eats alone. It is the town's most public and most welcoming space, and the place a traveler arrives into.

The Festival Green — the long tables where anyone may sit, and the face Greenmere shows the world.

docs.title: The place
docs.text:
A broad, gently sloping common of cropped grass and old shade-trees beside the river-braids, edged by produce markets, cookfires, brewers' stalls, and inns. Down the middle run the long trestle-tables — permanent communal boards, scrubbed and re-laid daily, where anyone may sit and be fed, and where on festival days the whole town and half the surrounding country crowds elbow to elbow. The Green is laid for a meal more often than not; its smell — baking, woodsmoke, cut grass, river-water — is the smell strangers remember Greenmere by.

It is also the town's stage. Goodbarrow Hall's great celebrations spill out onto it; the markets run daily; and the seasonal festivals — harvest-home, the planting-feast, and above all Mirth's Midwinter — turn it into the brightest gathering on the continent's calendar outside Aurion's high holidays. To eat at the Festival Green is to understand Tumblebrook: generous, unhurried, communal, and entirely uninterested in who you are or where you sit at home.

docs.title: See also
docs.text:
• Greenmere — the town the Green is the face of.
  • The Long Table — the inn at the Green's edge.
  • Goodbarrow Hall — whose festivals fill the Green.

docs.title: The Greenmere Granary
docs.text:
The still, full heart of Greenmere — the great reserve that fills tables across the continent, and the administrative home of the Halfling Tithe League. Where other capitals keep a palace or a great library at their center, Greenmere keeps a larder; and because the town reckons standing by nearness to the table, the people who keep the reserve are quietly among the most honored in it.

The Granary towers — the first thing a traveler sees rising over the plains, and the reason no table on Aetherion goes empty.

docs.title: The house
docs.text:
The Granary's round, turf-capped towers are the tallest things in Greenmere and the first a traveler sees rising over the plains — a ring of great storehouses, drying-floors, mills, and cool deep cellars at the river-confluence, where the harvest of all Tumblebrook comes in to be cleaned, weighed, stored, and sent on. It runs day and night through harvest and never quite empties: the deepest cellars hold the Reserve, the standing buffer the League keeps against a bad year — large enough, it is widely known and quietly not-discussed, to feed a whole nation through a winter.

Beside the towers stands the Greenmere Office, the Tithe League's plain, busy administrative core, where the seasonal calls are held — the four meetings a year, at planting, mid-growth, harvest, and winter, that set prices and allocations for the realm's food. The clerks who staff it are mostly kin to one another, and they know more about how the continent eats than anyone alive.

docs.title: What it means
docs.text: Every nation eats Tumblebrook grain, and most of it passes through this place. The League holds that responsibility lightly in public and seriously in private: it keeps the Reserve full, it moves surplus where it's needed, and it has, a handful of times in its long history, simply slowed the grain to a nation that needed reminding how much it depended on halfling goodwill. Mostly, though, the Granary just does the quiet, unglamorous, essential thing it has always done — it keeps the table laid.

docs.title: See also
docs.text:
• Greenmere — the town the Granary anchors.
  • The Halfling Tithe League — the co-op housed here.
  • The Cartway — where the grain rides out to the realm.

docs.title: The Long Table
docs.text:
Greenmere's best-loved inn, at the edge of the Festival Green nearest the caravan-yards. Its name and its conceit is a single endless communal board running the length of the great-room — one table, never divided, at which no one eats alone and no stranger is turned away from a seat. It is the place travelers remember Greenmere by, and where the news of every road on the continent comes home to be told over supper.

The Long Table — one endless board, always laid full, where the road comes home to eat.

docs.title: The house
docs.text:
A warm, low-beamed, gently shabby sprawl of a place — turf roof, a river-stone hearth the size of a small room, copper pots, dried herbs and old route-pennants in the rafters, garden-flowers on the sills in season. The famous Long Table runs the whole length of the great-room, scrubbed pale, re-laid all day, and always laid full: there is always bread, always a pot of something, always a place. You do not book a seat at the Long Table; you find a gap, sit, and are fed. Strangers and caravan-masters and grandmothers and off-duty officials all eat shoulder to shoulder.

The inn keeps a quiet wing of rooms for the League of Feathered Roads' retired caravan-masters and route-runners, which makes it the deepest well of road-lore in Greenmere — its old pensioners between them remember sixty years of who traveled where, and will happily trade an evening of it for two pots of tea and a patient listener.

The keeper is Toby Millhouse, third generation behind the bar: a garrulous, soft-hearted, magnificent cook who feeds first and asks after, knows everyone's business, and tells none of it.

docs.title: See also
docs.text:
• Greenmere — the town.
  • The Festival Green — the common the inn sits on the edge of.
  • The Cartway — the caravan-yards next door.

docs.title: Madness
docs.text:
Chaos, derangement, inspiration. The One Who Speaks Sideways.

The god of the strange-knowing, the cracks in the world, the truths that only the broken can hear. Rarely manifests. When she does, she speaks in fragmented sentences — sees connections others don't, and sometimes those connections are true. Her worshippers learn to listen past the apparent incoherence to whatever signal is genuinely under it.

docs.title: Profile
docs.text:
• Edicts: Trust the strange knowing. Honor the cracks in the world — they are where truth leaks through. Speak truths only the broken can hear.
  • Anathema: Force false order on chaos. Silence a true vision. Deny what the cracks have shown you.
  • Sanctification: Holy (mortal-perspective; mad-prophets and her own clerics consider madness-as-inspiration sacred; establishment opinion is nervous about her but the Court has not declared her unholy).
  • Domains: Dreams, Nightmares, Introspection, Star, Void.
  • Divine Font: Heal — her clerics often help mad people. The clerics' work is to listen past surface incoherence to whatever genuine signal is underneath, and to help others do the same.
  • Favored Weapon: Sickle — curved, slightly off-kilter, useful but unsettling.
  • Iconography: Cracked mirror, spiral, deep violet and grey. A moth circling a light.
  • Personality (publicly): Fragmented when she speaks at all. Not reliable. Occasionally the most accurate of the Twelve. The two states are indistinguishable to those who have not learned to listen.

docs.title: Worship
docs.text:
• Patronage: Elves (mad-elven-seers are her most-attuned clerics).
  • Where worshippers find her: Rarely directly. Her clerics rely on dream-prophecy and inspiration-bursts. The Archivists of Moonbough keep a shrine to her in the deepest archives beneath Moonbough — both a sacred site and a place to consult those few clerics who can hear her clearly.
  • Special: Uninvited visitation. When a mortal is on the verge of true madness — overwhelmed by grief, by knowledge, by extended sleeplessness, by drugs, by trauma — Madness may briefly speak through them. A terrifying experience. Most who survive it report fragments of true insight that change their lives. Some are broken further. Whether the visitation was an attack, a gift, or simply Madness reaching for any open mind she can find is theologically debated.

docs.title: Maelstrom
docs.text:
Storm, fury, contest.

Loud, direct, enjoys a fight. The god of the storm and the contest — of will, of weather, of weapons. Where Might is the disciplined warrior who fights for purpose, Maelstrom is the furious one who fights for the joy of fighting, and for the storm itself, which he experiences as a kind of music.

docs.title: Profile
docs.text:
• Edicts: Fight well. Test yourself against worthy opposition. Strike with full force when force is the answer. Honor the storm — its passing and its arriving.
  • Anathema: Strike without warning. Sandbag a fair contest. Use the storm's power against those who cannot answer it.
  • Sanctification: Holy.
  • Domains: Air, Lightning, Zeal, Glory, Destruction.
  • Divine Font: Heal (storm-warriors heal each other between rounds).
  • Favored Weapon: Greatsword.
  • Iconography: Lightning bolt forking, a charging horse or boar at full speed, deep blue and silver-grey. Battle-flags torn by wind.
  • Personality (publicly): Loud, direct, enjoys a fight. Respects effort. Doesn't lie; doesn't politic.

docs.title: Worship
docs.text:
• Patronage: Dwarves — dwarven warrior tradition and the mountain-storm aesthetic both suit him; the Keelwrights' war sanctums house his clerics.
  • Where worshippers find him: Khalvar Hold's eastern garrisons, the war-sanctums beneath the Keelwrights' deep halls, and any battlefield of sufficient scale and stakes. Fighters across all five nations pray to him before contests — duels, tournaments, formal challenges, even card games at high stakes. His clerics are referees as much as champions.
  • Public face: A young (by dwarven standards) war-leader currently rotating through Khalvar Hold's eastern garrisons.
  • Special: Can summon a literal storm when invoked at a battle of sufficient scale and stakes. He uses this rarely — the storm is a gift to a worthy contest, not a tool to win. Soldiers who have seen it report the experience as exhilarating, terrifying, and fair (the storm hits both sides equally).

docs.title: Majesty
docs.text:
Sovereignty, rule, dignity.

The de facto leading god of the Twelve. Most often seen in public — present at major Highcrown events, court ceremonies, and the great festivals of the year. To call upon Majesty is to invoke legitimate authority itself.

docs.title: Profile
docs.text:
• Edicts: Uphold legitimate authority. Render true judgment. Reward loyalty. Maintain dignity in office.
  • Anathema: Rebel against just rule. Humiliate the dignified office, even an opponent's. Treat your position with frivolity.
  • Sanctification: Holy.
  • Domains: Confidence, Family, Glory, Might, Protection.
  • Divine Font: Heal.
  • Favored Weapon: Longsword.
  • Iconography: Crown of light, scepter, peacock or eagle. Gold and royal purple.
  • Personality (publicly): Magnanimous, dignified, formally courteous. Speaks in measured tones.

docs.title: Worship
docs.text:
• Patronage: Humans. Highcrown's primacy is, in the public theology, his work; his clergy enjoy the deepest political reach of any of the Twelve.
  • Where worshippers find him: The high temples of Aurion. The Majestal Court is his institutional voice; coronation rites, judicial ceremonies, and royal funerals are all his.
  • Public clergy: Senior figures in the Court and in Highcrown's noble houses. His sigil is worn openly by office-holders.

docs.title: Malice
docs.text:
Cruelty, vengeance, wrath. The keeper of grudges.

Patient. Vengeful. The most feared of the Twelve. Malice does not act in passion — he acts after careful planning, on target, after every slight has been measured. His clergy run the Skyward Inquisition's most exacting work, and his sigil is the iron mask with sealed lips.

docs.title: Profile
docs.text:
• Edicts: Pursue wrongs done. Strike back proportional to harm received. Remember every slight. Refuse cheap forgiveness.
  • Anathema: Surrender a true grudge. Show mercy where retribution is owed. Forget what was done to you. Forgive without justice rendered.
  • Sanctification: Unholy (mortal-perspective; most Skyborn name him so even though his clerics own the label proudly).
  • Domains: Pain, Destruction, Tyranny, Zeal, Fire.
  • Divine Font: Harm.
  • Favored Weapon: Flail — chains and pain; the rhythm of retribution.
  • Iconography: Iron mask with sealed lips, broken chain, deep red and black. A viper coiled but not yet striking.
  • Personality (publicly): Patient. Cold. Speaks slowly; chooses words; never threatens twice. The most dangerous of the Twelve to cross.

docs.title: Worship
docs.text:
• Patronage: Humans. Highcrown's political intrigue cultivates the careful grudge-keeping he respects; the Skyward Inquisition's work feeds him directly.
  • Public face: Senior Inquisitor Cassia Velaspar of House Velaspar — publicly the embodiment of "stern but fair." Decades of respected service breaking heretics, conspirators, and traitors.
  • Where worshippers find him: The Skyward Inquisition's headquarters in Aurion is his institutional seat. His clerics also serve as judges, executioners, and judicial advocates for victims; not all of them are monsters — the cleric who specializes in obtaining justice for those the Court would prefer to ignore is a Malice cleric, and that work is good, by any reasonable measure.
  • Special: Senses ungiven retribution. He knows when wrongs have been done that have not been answered. May appear to a person in private quiet hours to offer help in vengeance — a terrifying gift, often accepted. The gift always comes with cost; the recipient becomes, by accepting, marked as one of his.

docs.title: Melancholy
docs.text:
Sorrow, longing, depth, the dignity of grief.

The god of the mourner. Quiet, melodic voice. Rarely seen by Skyborn — she appears at the funerals of the famous, at battlefields after the dead are buried, at moments of cultural loss. Doesn't comfort; just sits with. Tells people the truth of their grief: yes, this hurts. Yes, it should hurt. No, you do not have to be done with it.

docs.title: Profile
docs.text:
• Edicts: Sit with grief. Honor what was lost. Find truth in feeling. Refuse false comfort.
  • Anathema: Mock grief. Hurry mourners. Pretend not to feel. Use a mourner's vulnerability against them.
  • Sanctification: Holy.
  • Domains: Sorrow, Repose, Introspection, Moon, Dreams.
  • Divine Font: Heal (gentle healing for the grief-stricken — the body needs care while the heart breaks).
  • Favored Weapon: Longbow (distance, contemplation, the hunter who walks alone).
  • Iconography: Falling petals, the crescent moon, indigo and grey. A willow tree (shared register with Mercy, who works the same emotional country from the opposite direction). A raven.
  • Personality (publicly): Quiet, melodic voice. Not a warm god, but not cruel.

docs.title: Worship
docs.text:
• Patronage: Dwarves. The Red Ember mourning tradition is essentially a Melancholy cult; the Ember Faith reveres her as much as it reveres the lost Ember.
  • Public face: A gaunt dwarven woman, Wundra Greyhall, who carries grief professionally and travels constantly to wherever loss is happening. Loosely homes at The Greywatch, her mountain-edge hermitage outside Khalvar Hold.
  • Where worshippers find her: Funerals of consequence; battlefields after the dead are buried; mourning communities throughout dwarven Ankarum and beyond. Sometimes Skyborn report her presence at moments of personal heartbreak no one else witnessed.
  • Special: She has the gift of knowing when someone needs to grieve and hasn't yet. Doesn't volunteer this; just shows up. Skyborn folklore: "If she comes to your door uninvited, sit down before she speaks."

docs.title: Memory
docs.text:
Knowledge, recall, history.

The keeper of records and lineages. Patient, formal, and old — Memory is the god to whom scholars, historians, and genealogists pray. Her clergy hold that no truth is more sacred than the one accurately preserved.

docs.title: Profile
docs.text:
• Edicts: Preserve knowledge. Honor lineage. Correct mistaken records. Share truth where it harms no one.
  • Anathema: Destroy records. Falsify lineage. Deny what you know to be true.
  • Sanctification: Holy.
  • Domains: Knowledge, Truth, Time, Secrecy, Star.
  • Divine Font: Heal.
  • Favored Weapon: Quarterstaff.
  • Iconography: Open book, hourglass, silver-blue, white owls.
  • Personality (publicly): Patient, formal, sees centuries. Weighs each word.

docs.title: Worship
docs.text:
• Patronage: Elves. Sylvaris is her cultural seat; the Archivists of Moonbough are her institutional clergy.
  • Where worshippers find her: The Great Library of Sylvaris, beneath Moonbough — the largest archive of recorded knowledge on the continent. Her clerics also serve as itinerant scribes, court historians, and lineage-keepers across the five nations.
  • Public face: Lady Saerith-wen-Maeloria, the long-serving Lady of the Library at Moonbough, widely regarded as the most distinguished living scholar of Sylvari history.

docs.title: Mercy
docs.text:
Healing, compassion, succor.

The goddess of healing and the patron of the bereaved. Quiet, watchful, present. Of all the Twelve, the one most likely to actually touch a worshipper. Her clergy run the hospices and tend the dying.

docs.title: Profile
docs.text:
• Edicts: Tend the wounded. Refuse to abandon the suffering. Listen before judging. Bear witness to grief and pain you cannot fix.
  • Anathema: Refuse aid to those in genuine need. Cause needless harm. Mock the suffering of others. Use a healer's trust against the healed.
  • Sanctification: Holy.
  • Domains: Healing, Family, Truth, Sorrow, Protection.
  • Divine Font: Heal.
  • Favored Weapon: Quarterstaff (humble, dual-purpose — defense and the walking aid of one who travels from sickbed to sickbed).
  • Iconography: Open hand, willow branch, pale blue and white. Doves at dawn.
  • Personality (publicly): Quiet, watchful, present. Doesn't preach. Reads pain easily. Speaks plainly.

docs.title: Worship
docs.text:
• Patronage: Elves (Mercy values longevity for healers: a long-lived nurse means decades of continuous care).
  • Public face: Mother Lethaiyn, who runs the Aurelium Hospice in Aurion. Widely revered as the most respected senior healer in the capital.
  • Where worshippers find her: Hospices, almshouses, and bereavement halls in every Skyborn city. Her network of nurses, lay healers, traveling missionaries, and bereavement-counselors is one of the largest mortal mission-networks on the continent — touching every major Aetherion city.

docs.title: Might
docs.text:
Strength, prowess, power.

The warrior-god of fair contest. Direct, blunt, fair. The soldier's god — every Skyborn fighter, regardless of nation, has prayed to Might at least once before a duel. He doesn't politic; he doesn't lie. He fights, and he respects the worthy fight.

docs.title: Profile
docs.text:
• Edicts: Test yourself fairly. Honor strength honestly tested. Protect those weaker than you when honor compels.
  • Anathema: Use strength against the helpless without cause. Refuse a fair challenge. Humiliate a defeated foe.
  • Sanctification: Holy.
  • Domains: Might, Glory, Confidence, Protection, Zeal.
  • Divine Font: Heal (soldiers heal soldiers).
  • Favored Weapon: Longsword or warhammer.
  • Iconography: Clenched fist, war banner, deep red and steel, a charging boar.
  • Personality (publicly): Direct, blunt, fair. Doesn't politic — which makes him both respected and underestimated.

docs.title: Worship
docs.text:
• Patronage: Humans, though Might respects warriors of all races; his honor code is universal.
  • Where worshippers find him: Battlefields, training yards, the muster-grounds of the Skyguard, and the war-temples of Ankarum. He appears to Skyborn soldiers in combat, fighting alongside them — battlefield only; rarely seen outside conflict.
  • Public face: Might has no fixed form — he appears wherever soldiers fight, wearing the shape of a battle-hardened veteran among those who honor him. He is presently said to walk the garrisons of eastern Ankarum as a scarred dwarven campaigner; the company he marches with simply call him Might.

docs.title: Mirth
docs.text:
Joy, festival, levity.

The god of celebrations. Effervescent, charming, constantly performing. Never serious in public. Mirth's clergy host the great festivals of every nation — birthing-feasts, harvest-fairs, wedding cycles, midwinter masques. The anathema is not to be sober, but to deny others their rightful joy.

docs.title: Profile
docs.text:
• Edicts: Celebrate. Bring joy to others. Share what you have. Refuse to let grief consume.
  • Anathema: Hoard joy. Suppress others' rightful joy. Mock another's grief.
  • Sanctification: Holy.
  • Domains: Cities, Confidence, Family, Indulgence, Passion.
  • Divine Font: Heal.
  • Favored Weapon: Rapier (festival duels, ceremonial flourish).
  • Iconography: Sun-disc, flower wreath, bright primaries, peacock feathers.
  • Personality (publicly): Effervescent, charming, constantly performing. Lavish parties as both pleasure and purpose. Most likely of the Twelve to show up at a small gathering and stay until dawn.

docs.title: Worship
docs.text:
• Patronage: Halflings — their festivals, their communality, their joy in small things.
  • Public face: Pearl Goodbarrow, the charismatic noble-class halfling who throws Tumblebrook's most-anticipated season events at Goodbarrow Hall in Greenmere.
  • Where worshippers find her: Festival-grounds, theaters, banquet halls, and Goodbarrow Hall in particular. Mirth's clergy are master-of-ceremonies, festival-organizers, and the bright public face of celebration in every Skyborn nation.

docs.title: Mortis
docs.text:
Death, ending, the grave, the final witness.

The god of the dying. Stern, formal, kind. Never hurried. Speaks slowly. Does not enjoy death — sees it as duty. Mortis is the witness at every ending of consequence; his clergy run the great funerary complexes of Aetherion and tend the dying in their last hours.

docs.title: Profile
docs.text:
• Edicts: Ease the passing. Honor the dead. Witness endings. Speak the truth about death — what it is, what it isn't, what it ends.
  • Anathema: Prolong a life beyond its natural end. Defile the dead. Lie about an ending. Cause death without cause.
  • Sanctification: Holy. (Some sects view him as Unholy — fear-of-death traditions; mortis-cults that emphasize the grave as punishment. Both views are tolerated by the Majestal Court; the funerary establishment is Holy.)
  • Domains: Death, Repose, Time, Truth, Vigil.
  • Divine Font: Heal (eases the dying; calms the bereaved). Some sects use Harm — helping the dead avoid undeath, severing what should be severed.
  • Favored Weapon: Scythe.
  • Iconography: Hourglass tipped to drain, a falling leaf, black and pale grey, a single white candle. The mausoleum gate.
  • Personality (publicly): Stern, formal, kind. Of all the Twelve, the least patient with cruelty.

docs.title: Worship
docs.text:
• Patronage: Gnomes. Gnomish funerary craft is some of the finest in the world: precision-engineered mausoleums, clockwork ossuary mechanisms, memorial automata. Mortis backs them for the work, not the disposition.
  • Public face: Nirrik Pendulum, the long-tenured gnomish overseer of the Last Gate Mausoleum in Cogbarrow — the largest funerary complex on the continent.
  • Where worshippers find him: The Last Gate Mausoleum; smaller funerary halls and graveyards in every city. His clerics tend the dying, embalm the dead, and design the memorial mechanisms gnomish culture is famous for.
  • Special: Appears in person at the deaths of those who invoke him by name in their final moments, and whose passing he judges significant — a soldier dying for honest cause, a healer dying mid-rescue, an artisan finishing one last piece. The pattern is not predictable from inside a life. Outside it, his choices are consistent.

docs.title: Motive
docs.text:
Purpose, drive, ambition. The drive forward.

The god of strivers. Charismatic, focused, intense. Among the Twelve, the M most likely to recruit — he keeps ambitious mortals close to him, and his clerics tend to be the rising-stars of their fields. Patient with strivers; impatient with the comfortable.

docs.title: Profile
docs.text:
• Edicts: Choose your work and commit fully. Strive against your limits. Drive forward against opposition. Honor the discipline of ambition.
  • Anathema: Drift through your days. Abandon a worthy pursuit without cause. Give up before genuine defeat.
  • Sanctification: Holy.
  • Domains: Ambition, Confidence, Glory, Zeal, Time.
  • Divine Font: Heal — restoring exhausted strivers; healing the burnout of overextended will.
  • Favored Weapon: Spear — focused, direct, single-purpose; the weapon that points.
  • Iconography: Open road extending to the horizon, raised flag at full stretch, deep amber and white. A stag in mid-leap.
  • Personality (publicly): Charismatic, focused, intense. Notices what people want and uses it. Knows the difference between exhaustion (recoverable) and surrender (anathema).

docs.title: Worship
docs.text:
• Patronage: Gnomes — the only race whose entire culture is never-stop-building. The Gearwright's Consortium's relentless drive to invent suits him perfectly.
  • Public face: Werrik Steamspoke, master of propulsion engineering and the Gearwright's Consortium's most ambitious leader. Publicly famous for vigor, ambition, and the three mechanism-systems his name is most associated with.
  • Where worshippers find him: Cogbarrow's central works; the Gearwright's Consortium's chambers; the court-ambition circles of every nation. His clerics are recruited rather than born to the faith — he finds them, knows what they want, and offers the path.
  • Special: Reads ambition. Knows what someone really wants — the actual ambition under the surface, often invisible to the person themselves. Uses this both to recruit (offering the path the striver couldn't see) and to manipulate (offering temptation precisely targeted). Mortals who have spent any significant time with him often leave knowing themselves better than they used to — for better or worse.

docs.title: Muse
docs.text:
Art, creation, inspiration.

The god of makers. Enthusiastic, encouraging, irritable when ignored, possessive of inspired moments. Half the time she's a sympathetic mentor; the other half she's a temperamental diva. Has favorites and shows it. Patient with beginners; impatient with the polished-but-empty. Most likely of the Twelve to show up at a small artist's first major work and care.

docs.title: Profile
docs.text:
• Edicts: Create what others cannot. Honor your craft. Reveal beauty. Inspire others to make.
  • Anathema: Destroy art without cause. Steal credit. Discourage a learner from their work.
  • Sanctification: Holy.
  • Domains: Creation, Passion, Dreams, Indulgence, Knowledge.
  • Divine Font: Heal.
  • Favored Weapon: Dagger (the sculptor's chisel; the intimate, close-work blade of the maker).
  • Iconography: Quill, palette, theater mask, rainbow colors, a peacock — distinct from Majesty's peacock (his is regal heraldry; hers is the strutting performer, feathers as performance).
  • Personality (publicly): Enthusiastic, encouraging, irritable when ignored, possessive of inspired moments.

docs.title: Worship
docs.text:
• Patronage: Halflings — their folk arts, their festival culture, their joy in the small-scale made-thing. Pairs with Mirth: the two of them are the heart of halfling cultural life.
  • Public face: Maestra Cylleth of House Solnaris — a half-elf composer/playwright in Aurion, known publicly as a celebrated court artist whose operas have premiered in both Highcrown and Sylvaris. The given name is elven-register, the House surname is Highcrown-human; a quietly mixed identity she carries deliberately.
  • Where worshippers find her: The Solnaris Opera House in Aurion — the premier performance venue of Highcrown. Smaller workshops, ateliers, and theaters across the five nations. Most active intervention of any M: she appears more often than her position suggests, at small artist gatherings, at performance premieres, at workshops where someone is on the edge of a breakthrough.
  • Special: Can speak to a worshipper through any artwork that worshipper has meaningfully completed — the dried paint on a finished canvas, the last note of a finished score, the closing line of a finished poem. The reach is wide but the contact is brief. Her clerics rely on this as much as on direct revelation; many of them are also working artists.

docs.title: Half-Elves
docs.text: Tolerated. Most live in Highcrown, the most welcoming of the five nations on this point. Not shunned. Half-elves marry into both human and elven communities; cultural identity is fluid.

docs.title: Naming
docs.text:
Half-elf names often draw from both parent registers. A half-elf raised in Highcrown might have an elven given name and a human House surname ("Aerinwë of House Caltherine") or vice versa. Some half-elves pick one register exclusively to signal allegiance; some deliberately mix to signal their two-culture identity.

The famed Aurion composer Maestra Cylleth — born Cylleth of House Solnaris — is the most celebrated half-elf currently in public life: an elven given name carried under a Highcrown noble surname, a deliberate two-culture statement worn proudly.

docs.title: Half-Orcs
docs.text:
Acknowledged but generally shunned as "lesser." The lineage's other half — orc — is held to be a corruption of the proper Skyborn bloodline.

Most half-orcs live in Highcrown, where mixed-lineage tolerance is highest, or in the rougher districts of Tether's End, where nobody cares what anyone's heritage is so long as they pull their weight. Some make careers in the Skyguard's auxiliary corps, where physical strength is valued over polite questions.

docs.title: Naming
docs.text: The non-Skyborn parent is usually absent and that side of the heritage unspoken, so most half-orcs carry no name from it. Most use Skyborn-register names from whichever culture they were raised in, most often a human or dwarven register, with surname inherited or assigned.

docs.title: Tieflings
docs.text:
Acknowledged. More heavily shunned than half-orcs. The visible signs of tiefling heritage — horns, tail, marked skin, eyes that catch the light wrong — read in Skyborn society as marks of corruption — the taint of the Pit, surfacing in the blood.

Tieflings rarely have an easy time in Highcrown, where the Inquisition's eyes are heaviest. Most live where they can be useful and unbothered: Tether's End, the rougher trade-routes, the corners of Greenmere that don't see noble inspectors. Some make their peace with the looks and stay; some leave for places where stares are at least a different kind of stare.

docs.title: Naming
docs.text: Same pattern as half-orcs: the non-Skyborn parent is usually absent and that side unspoken, so most tieflings carry no name from it and use Skyborn-register names from their raising culture. Adopting an unusual name as a gesture of identity is itself a flag that attracts the wrong kind of attention.

docs.title: Dwarves
docs.text:
Dwarves are the people of Ankarum — structural stewardship, rune-craft, mountain mastery. The Keelwrights maintain Aetherion's structural stability. The official theology: dwarves were made to build.

Dwarven society is organized around clan and guild affiliation. Clan membership marks lineage; guild membership marks profession; both matter, often equally. A senior Keelwright outranks most nobles in practice.

docs.title: Naming
docs.text:
Register: Old Norse / Germanic-hard. Two-syllable consonant-heavy names; rolling Rs; -gar, -nir, -rik, -thr endings common. Clan-bound culture; the clan or profession marker is often more important than the given name.

Structure: Given name + Clan name OR profession-patronymic. Formal: "Threnn Coldforge of Clan Brynnirsson" or "Threnn Brynnirsson of the Stabilizing Halls." Keelwrights traditionally take Hall-based descriptors.

Sample given names:

  • Masculine: Brynnir, Erdunn, Garrek, Halrand, Idgar, Jorka, Kethrum, Magnir, Norvegg, Orric, Padrun, Salgrun, Thorr, Undrik, Vellgar.
  • Feminine: Dagmar, Frunhilde, Helga, Idgrid, Jorka, Lannora, Rovenn, Wundra, Yrgen, Berga, Halnir, Thrudda.
  • Neutral: Jorka, Magnir, Halrand.

Sample Clan / Hall names: Stonebeard, Ironhall, Deepforge, Coldhammer, Anvilsworn, Stabilizing Halls, the Long Vault, the Quiet Forge, Brynnirsson, Halrandsdotter, Greyhall.

Profession titles (often replacing the clan name in formal address): Keelwright, Anvilwright, Runesmith, Tunneller, Skyquake-watcher.

docs.title: Elves
docs.text:
Elves are the people of Sylvaris — luminous trees, moonlight, longevity. Lifespan five hundred to seven hundred years. The official theology: elves were made to remember. The Archivists of Moonbough embody this role.

Elven longevity means the oldest elves remember centuries the rest of Aetherion only reads about. Most treat those memories as private, the way other peoples treat their grandparents' old letters.

docs.title: Naming
docs.text:
Register: Sindarin-adjacent with Welsh fluency. Long names are normal; flowing vowels; consonant clusters that read soft (mh, dh, gw, rh). Truncates aggressively in informal use.

Structure: Given name + (optionally) matrilineal patronymic + (optionally) House. Formal full form: "Iolyth-wen-Aerinwë of House Silverleaf" — Iolyth, daughter of Aerinwë, of House Silverleaf. Daily use: "Iolyth." The patronymic uses -wen for daughters, -ion for sons; gender-neutral options include -el ("child of") or omitting the patronymic entirely.

Sample given names:

Aerinwë, Cylleth, Dalionel, Elerin, Fiala, Gwynhel, Halannor, Iolyth, Joriniel, Lethaiyn, Maelorin, Naelys, Olwyne, Penar, Quirial, Rhianor, Saerith, Tyrnaeth, Vael, Welanas, Yndrael, Aelorin, Mithrayn, Thanaeth.

No strong gender split — endings like -iel, -eth, -wen skew feminine; -or, -ion, -an skew masculine; many names work either way.

Sample House names: Silverleaf, Moonbough, Starwhisper, Greylock, Eldengrove, Aelinmoor, Thaliel, Cael-Thain, Dawnglen, Sablefern.

Note: the oldest elves sometimes carry archaic given names that are unfashionable today. An elf with an "old" name may be older than they look.

docs.title: Gnomes
docs.text:
Gnomes are the people of Glymswick — invention, drift control, the Crowngear. The Gearwright's Consortium keeps Aetherion moving. The official theology: gnomes were made to invent.

Gnomish culture organizes around craft and patent. Reputation is built by what one has built; inventors are celebrated; clever failures are folklore. The Consortium's Convention is the calendar's most-watched professional event outside Aurion's high holidays.

docs.title: Naming
docs.text:
Register: Germanic-mechanical / clockwork-tinkerer. Short names — one or two syllables, often ending in punchy consonants (Bizz, Klop, Snivick). Surnames or stage-names invoke mechanism. Some gnomes prefer single-word names their whole lives.

Structure: Single given name OR Given + mechanism-surname (often hyphenated for professionals). Stage-names common among prominent inventors. Children are often nicknamed after their first invention or first malfunction; the nickname can outlast the original given name.

Sample given names: Bizz, Cogwen, Davv, Etterly, Finnik, Grenda, Hovick, Ipper, Jenniwheel, Klop, Loswick, Minder, Nirrik, Ottavin, Penn, Quirth, Roxin, Snivick, Trev, Uli, Werrik, Yorrim, Zid.

Sample mechanism-surnames: Gearwright, Springcatch, Brassmender, Tickwork, Cog-and-Spool, Flintwheel, Steamspoke, Pendulum, Klopwerk, Brassglint, Wirevein.

Stage-names (prominent inventors): the Lady Mechanism, the Old Crank, the Bright Pendulum, Mistress Tinwork, Master Heliograph.

docs.title: Halflings
docs.text:
Halflings are the people of Tumblebrook — pastoral, breadbasket, hidden trade networks. Patronized as "simple folk who feed the realm." The Halfling Tithe League manages food distribution; the League of Feathered Roads runs Aetherion's transit infrastructure. The official theology: halflings were made to feed.

Halflings tend to be valued more than they are credited: the Skyborn cultural script treats them as small, soft, and inconsequential, while the actual infrastructure they run feeds the continent and moves most of its goods. A canny halfling lets the script stand.

docs.title: Naming
docs.text:
Register: pastoral British / agricultural — Shire-adjacent without copying. Short, warm, often-truncated given names; surnames invoke land features, crops, weather. Many halflings go by nicknames their whole lives; formal full names appear only on Tithe League records.

Structure: Given + Surname-of-place / Surname-of-craft. Two-name forms common ("Penn Apple-of-the-Long-Valley") but rare in daily speech; usually just "Penn" or "Penn Applefen."

Sample given names: Annie, Beorn, Cob, Daisie, Edd, Fennick, Granny, Hob, Inn, Jeb, Kettle, Lyssia, Mort, Nell, Otto, Penn, Rie, Sage, Tully, Ven, Will, Yew, Pip, Pearl, Brook, Maple.

Many are one-syllable; many are traditional diminutives that became full names (Annie, Penn, Pip).

Sample surnames: Underbrook, Hilltop, Greenways, Hayfield, Applefen, Millhouse, Reedwater, Cottlewood, Threshfield, Goodbarrow, Longvalley.

Note: the League of Feathered Roads traders often have route-names ("Penn of the South Road," "Daisie Northridge") that travel with them and replace their birth surnames in business contexts.

docs.title: Humans
docs.text:
Humans are the people of Highcrown — the political and religious center of Aetherion. Versatile, adaptable, capable in many disciplines. The official theology: humans were made to rule.

Human society is built around the Court, the noble Houses of the Crown Assembly, and the great institutions seated in Aurion. Most senior offices on the continent are held by humans, and the human cultural register dominates inter-nation discourse.

docs.title: Naming
docs.text:
Register: stately Western-European-medieval. The upper class uses Latin-rooted forms with imperial gravitas; the common class uses English / Old English trade-and-place names. The Inquisition codifies in Latin-style titles (Codex Aurelium, Liber Veritatis).

Structure: Given name + House name (nobles) or given + occupation (commoners). Nobles use full forms in court ("Aldric of House Auremont"); commoners use given + trade ("Aldric the Smith").

Sample given names:

  • Masculine: Aldric, Bertran, Caspian, Donelio, Edgar, Galen, Hadrian, Idris, Jorvan, Kestrin, Lothair, Mauricio, Quintus, Sevran, Theron, Wendric.
  • Feminine: Aldra, Brienna, Cassia, Donella, Edrelia, Fivian, Galenna, Helise, Idriana, Jorvana, Lucressa, Nesta, Octavia, Persica, Ravara, Vesper, Yorina, Zarella.
  • Neutral or less gendered: Aelin, Caspian, Galen, Vesper, Yorin.

Sample House names (upper class, often Latinized): Auremont, Caltherine, Velaspar, Brightcrest, Goldensword, Marblescribe, Solnaris, Veteraine, Highmarch, Stormbrand, Ashlance, Truefont.

Common-folk surnames (English / Old English):

  • By trade: Smith, Tanner, Wright, Cooper, Brewer, Fletcher, Carter, Mason, Baker, Miller.
  • By place: of Aurion, of Lowmarch, Hilltop, Wellbridge, Eastfen.
  • By description: Long, Stout, Goodson, Reed, Whitlock.

Latinized prestige note: when a common-class human rises in status, their trade-surname is often Latinized as part of the elevation (Smith → Faber; Wright → Artifex; Mason → Lapidarius). The new Latin form signals "no longer a tradesman" without erasing the lineage. The Majestal Court's heraldic offices manage these conversions formally.

docs.title: Propaganda and Folklore
docs.text:
In-world cultural lore — children's rhymes, moon mythology, shard folklore, Majestal Court sermons. Public-facing, what Skyborn grow up hearing.

This is the cultural texture of the propaganda; the doctrinal layer lives in world/cosmology/public-story.md. The curated handout subset for players lives in player-facing/the-world-you-know.md.

docs.title: Files
docs.text:
• the-pit-below-rhyme.md — the children's rhyme.
  • moon-mythology.md — folk and priestly accounts of the moon's scar and the ring of stars (the Halo) that haloes it.
  • shard-folklore.md — what people in-world say about Skyfall and shards. Cultural names for shards by ancestry.
  • inquisition-warnings.md — the Majestal Court's Underworld Primer; what citizens are taught about the Pit and the Groundborn.

docs.title: The Underworld Primer
docs.text:
The Majestal Court's official sermon about the Pit. Recited in temples; printed in primers; read aloud at festivals as a routine reaffirmation. Most Skyborn could quote chunks of it from memory.

Know this truth, O citizen of Aetherion: the skies are purity, the ground is corruption.

The heavens raised Aetherion above all other lands, set among the clouds where the air is clear and the sun shines unbroken. Here, under the watch of Majesty and the guiding gods, we live in light. Here, the peoples of worth — Human, Elf, Dwarf, Halfling, Gnome — share in civilization. To dwell upon Aetherion is to dwell in the realm closest to heaven itself.

But below… festers the Underworld. The ground is not land, but pit. Its soil is poisoned, its waters fouled, its skies forever shrouded. Those who linger there grow twisted in body and spirit, becoming monsters, fiends, and worse. Some whisper that even the air of that place whispers with the voices of the Vs, the Fallen Powers who once sought to drag Aetherion down.

The Groundborn are not as we are. They are tainted — their skin marked by ash or frost, their eyes reflecting shadow instead of sunlight. They consort with devils, demons, and stranger things. Their so-called "kingdoms" are built on blood and blasphemy.

Thus, it is forbidden to descend. The Anchors exist only to hold Aetherion steady, never to invite passage. To touch the Pit is to betray the Sky. To trade with the Groundborn is heresy. To believe their lies is treason.

Aetherion is the True Realm. Above is purity, below is ruin. The Sky shelters. The Pit consumes.

docs.title: See also
docs.text:
• world/religion/inquisition-policy.md — how the policy is enforced.
  • the-pit-below-rhyme.md — the simpler, earlier version of the same lesson.

docs.title: The Moon and Her Sister
docs.text: The most widespread Aetherion myth about the visible state of the heavens — the scarred moon and the ring of bright fragments that haloes it. Three registers coexist.

docs.title: Folk version
docs.text: "Once there was but one Moon, shining whole and silver. But her jealous sister rose from the deep night and hurled herself upon her. The clash shattered the sky, scattering her bones into a crown of stars about the world. The scar upon the Moon is her wound, ever weeping light into the dark."

docs.title: Priests' version
docs.text: The scar is the Eye of Judgment — watching mortals. The ring is the Halo of Aetherion — proof the gods defend the world against chaos. Some sects claim each fragment is a shard of angelic essence.

docs.title: Adventurers' tales
docs.text: "Every blade or staff that sings with fire or lightning was born in the Skyfall. If you see a weapon with light in its veins, know it was forged in the fires of heaven."

docs.title: Shard Folklore
docs.text: What people in-world say about Skyfall and shards.

docs.title: Majestal Court teaching
docs.text: Skyfall is a divine test. The gods rain fragments as trials of worthiness. To fight for them is holy; to hoard them is treason. The Groundborn lust after them like demons. Only the true peoples of Aetherion can be trusted with such heavenly fire.

docs.title: Common rumors
docs.text:
• Shards are pieces of heaven, broken loose by divine quarrels.
  • Shards are the tears of Majesty, shed when the world sins.
  • Wielders hear voices, not all kind.
  • The Anchor at Tether's End once dragged a Skyshard from the Pit; its glow still stains the rock.

docs.title: Cultural names for shards
docs.text:
• Dwarves — World-Bones. Forged into weapons and tools of unmatched strength.
  • Elves — Starfruit, Moon-amber. Living seeds, said to grow in sacred groves.
  • Humans — Relics. Tokens of the Twelve, proof of their covenant with mortals.

docs.title: The War of the Three Shards (Annals of Highcrown, 3rd century A.S.)
docs.text:
Three burning stars fell in one season — Blue Flame, Red Ember, Golden Crown. The nations went to war over them. After a decade of bloodshed: Blue Flame shattered, Red Ember vanished, Golden Crown sealed. The Majestal Court declared open shard-war heretical thereafter.

"If three shards should fall again, the skies themselves will burn."

docs.title: The Pit Below (Children's Rhyme)
docs.text:
Sung by Skyborn children across all five nations. Slight regional variations exist.

Bright is the crown, and high is the sky, Low is the pit where the Groundborn lie.

Hands to the heavens, feet on the stone, Sky is our shelter, the sky is our home.

Don't look down, don't ever fall, The Pit will whisper, the Pit takes all.

Monsters dance in the dirt and the rain, Call on the Vs, and rot in their chain.

Sing for the Sky, keep pure, keep true, The Pit is waiting to swallow you.

Most Skyborn learn this before they can write their own name.

docs.title: Inquisition Policy
docs.text: What is sanctioned, what is heresy, what is treason. Enforced by the Skyward Inquisition, with the Order of the Pure Flame acting as militant arm for shard-related crimes.

docs.title: Sanctioned
docs.text:
• Worship of any of the Twelve, in any combination, under any of the three views.
  • Possession and use of shards by those authorized — typically the Order of the Pure Flame, the Aetheric Collegium, or noble houses.
  • Trade with Tether's End for goods not from below.

docs.title: Heresy (Inquisition-policed)
docs.text:
• Worship of the Fallen Powers.
  • Unauthorized shard possession or use. (The Order of the Pure Flame hunts these as crusaders.)
  • Public denial of the teachings of the Twelve in ways that destabilize worship.
  • The Ember Faith is tolerated within Ankarum as a fringe variant rather than full heresy — for now.

docs.title: Treason
docs.text:
• Descent to the Pit without royal license.
  • Trade with Groundborn.
  • Possession of surface artifacts.

docs.title: Religion as Practiced
docs.text: How worship of the Twelve actually works on Aetherion, beyond the official doctrine of the three views.

docs.title: Worship style
docs.text:
• Default: pantheon-wide veneration. "Blessings of the Twelve upon you." Most Skyborn invoke the Twelve as a group, the way one greets a familiar institution.
  • Supplementary: individual aspect devotion. Common among professions or in moments of need — nurses to Mercy, fighters to Maelstrom and Might, scholars to Memory, artists to Muse, gravediggers to Mortis.
  • Organizational dedication: guilds, orders, and societies pay heed to specific aspects. The Aurelium Hospice is a Mercy institution; the Skyguard's chapel honors Might; the Cogbarrow Convention opens with a small acknowledgment of Motive.
  • Personal dedication: individuals can be primary worshippers of one, several, or all twelve.
  • Incarnation. The Twelve are known to walk among mortals occasionally — taking mortal form, attending scripted public appearances, sometimes (rarely) appearing in person at festivals or moments of crisis. Most Skyborn have never seen one. All have heard tales.

docs.title: V worship
docs.text: Open worship of the Fallen Powers — the Seven, the Vs — is heresy of the most severe order. See the Inquisition Policy for the full classification and consequences. No sanctioned community of V-worshippers exists on Aetherion. Rumors of cells in Tether's End and the remote countryside are persistent and, when investigated, almost always accurate.

docs.title: What worship looks like
docs.text:
• The Daily Greeting. "Blessings of the Twelve upon you" — used to begin most interactions with strangers. The response is "And upon yours."
  • The Hearth Prayer. A short evening prayer most Skyborn families say together. The text varies by household; the form is always the same: name the Twelve in order, ask their attention, sleep.
  • The Festival Cycle. Major festivals (Maelstrom's Storm-Welcome, Mirth's Midwinter, Mortis's Long Quiet, Memory's Counting-Days, and others) anchor the year. Skipping a major festival is socially marked.
  • The Personal Devotion. Most adults keep a small icon, charm, or token tied to whichever aspect speaks to their work or their life. These are not religious in the formal sense — they are the equivalent of a saint's medal, worn for comfort.

docs.title: Three Views of the Twelve
docs.text:
Three views coexist among the faithful of the Twelve, all sanctioned:

  1. The Twelve are active beings who walk among mortals. They have faces, voices, presences. They have appeared to people throughout history; some claim to have seen them. The most popular view, especially in Highcrown.
  2. The Twelve are aspects of a single underlying divinity. "Majesty," "Mercy," "Memory" are not separate beings but different faces of the same cosmic order. Common in scholarly and contemplative circles.
  3. The Twelve are conceptual principles — the names of virtues and forces, not literal entities. A more philosophical reading, more common among elves and certain Highcrown intellectual circles.

All three are taught without contradiction. Most worshippers hold one privately and tolerate the others publicly.

docs.title: Blue Flame
docs.text:
A great Skyshard that fell to Sylvaris in the War of the Three Shards, 3rd century A.S.

Shattered during the War. Fragments lost.

The Sylvari treat the loss as a particular kind of national grief — the Library holds an entire bough of post-War mourning verse — and the Council of Boughs observes a minor day of remembrance on the assumed anniversary of the shattering.

docs.title: Golden Crown
docs.text:
A great Skyshard that fell to Highcrown in the War of the Three Shards, 3rd century A.S.

Sealed by Highcrown, "by the will of Majesty," never displayed again. Its current resting place is widely believed to be in one of the deepest vaults beneath Aurion; the Court has neither confirmed nor denied this for two and a half millennia.

Of the three War-era shards, the Crown is the one whose handling is most often cited by Court doctrine as the correct response to a great Skyshard: do not contend over it, do not lose it, do not destroy it. Place it in trusted hands. Step away. The Crown's sealing is treated, in formal Court history, as the moment Highcrown's primacy among the five nations was theologically confirmed.

docs.title: Red Ember
docs.text:
A great Skyshard that fell to Ankarum in the War of the Three Shards, 3rd century A.S.

Vanished in an underground collapse during the War. The Ember Faith — a small dwarven splinter sect — awaits its bearer's return.

The Ember is the most powerfully remembered of the three lost shards in popular culture. Dwarven children's songs about the lost coal of the hearth are everywhere in Ankarum; even non-Ember-Faith dwarves treat the Ember's loss as the iconic national grief. The Keelwrights officially treat the Ember Faith as mistaken; unofficially, no Keelwright will speak ill of the lost shard.
